items.title: Call to Order
Roll Call
Welcome
Pledge of Allegiance
Changes to the Agenda
Approval of the Agenda
Public Comment

publicComments.name: Kristi Herrera
publicComments.summary: A 24-year kindergarten teacher at Hoover described the challenges of her class of 27 students (33% with IEPs, 63% multilingual learners, 37% with elevated behavioral needs) and urged the board to address large class sizes to support both students and teachers.

publicComments.name: Sandy Hoover
publicComments.summary: A retired Redwood City School teacher and former kindergarten instructor urged the board to examine large kindergarten class sizes and slow timelines for identifying and assessing students with special needs, arguing both issues affect every school in the district.

publicComments.name: Erin Washburn
publicComments.summary: The Redwood City Teachers Association Membership Chair announced that RCTA unanimously voted to endorse the Redwood City School District Parcel Tax, praising its equitable per-square-foot structure and $12.2 million annual benefit.

publicComments.name: Thomas Rogerson
publicComments.summary: A parent of children at Henry Ford and North Star urged the board to prioritize recruiting and retaining high-quality teachers, and to consider equity and teacher retention impacts of class size decisions, particularly for schools serving more demanding student populations.

publicComments.name: Aracely Flores Mendoza
publicComments.summary: A second-year education specialist at Hoover described how high caseloads and minimal aide support make it extremely difficult to provide individualized IEP services, and respectfully asked the board to provide two aides for Hoover resource teachers next year.

publicComments.name: Ann Briljafa
publicComments.summary: A current TK teacher shared data showing that approximately 10% of TK students have IEPs, 6% have SSTs initiated, and 11% have difficult behavioral needs, and urged the board to keep kindergarten class sizes as small as possible given the increasing complexity of student needs.

publicComments.name: Lisbeth Estrada and Jesús Ponce
publicComments.summary: Hoover parents speaking in Spanish (with English translation provided) thanked the board for investments at Hoover, and urged prioritization of extended TK hours, expansion of TK at Hoover, and instructional assistants in kindergarten and first grade.

publicComments.name: Heather McCormick
publicComments.summary: A Henry Ford parent and classroom volunteer described the challenges of her third-grader's class of 32 students, noting that physical space constraints, classroom management demands, and students being left behind are concerns, and expressed hope for smaller classes next year.

publicComments.name: Augustine Espino
publicComments.summary: A parent at Kennedy School expressed concern about large class sizes leaving students behind, shared his community outreach efforts in support of the parcel tax, and stressed the importance of accountability to the Latino community over the tax's 8-year term.

publicComments.name: Pam Barash
publicComments.summary: A 36-year kindergarten teacher at Henry Ford described the dramatic increase in student needs over her career, warned that a class size of 30 would make meaningful teaching impossible, and urged the board not to allow Redwood City to have the largest kindergarten class size in San Mateo County.

publicComments.name: Lilia Amalia Valdez-Monet
publicComments.summary: A Hoover parent since 2017 who volunteers regularly in Miss Herrera's kindergarten class described firsthand the impact of having only one Spanish kindergarten class instead of two, and asked the board to reduce class sizes and implement a second Spanish kinder class.

publicComments.name: Patricia Renderos
publicComments.summary: A TK parent speaking in Spanish expressed excitement about positive learning results she was seeing in TK, said she was spreading the word for more children to enroll, and asked the board to give strong support to kindergarten teachers.

publicComments.name: Sophia Santos
publicComments.summary: A first-grade teacher at Hoover in her first year with the district described feeling overwhelmed managing 27 students with diverse behaviors and needs, and asked the board to take steps to reduce class sizes to better support all students.

publicComments.name: Jackie Moore
publicComments.summary: A special education teacher and Hoover parent who did an interdistrict transfer for her twins advocated for smaller class sizes, a paraeducator in every TK-through-2 classroom, and a paraeducator for each resource teacher, emphasizing that the entry into education should be a positive experience.

publicComments.name: Rajanesh Patarath
publicComments.summary: A U.S. Marine Corps veteran and father of a student in Ms. Herrera's kindergarten class at Hoover praised Ms. Herrera's teaching and asked the board to consider the impact of class size on students and teachers, expressing concern about any potential increase.

items.title: Labor Association Comments

publicComments.name: Brenna Gear
publicComments.summary: President of the Redwood City Teachers Association asked the board to officially endorse the Prop 55 extension and announced that over the next several board meetings, RCTA would be sharing data and teacher stories on negotiation topics including class size, salary, and safety.

publicComments.name: Unidentified speaker (CSEA representative)
publicComments.summary: A classified employees association representative raised concerns about pay inequity between classified employees, teachers, and administrators, arguing that equal percentage raises provide vastly different dollar amounts and urged the board to consider truly fair and equitable salary increases for classified staff.

publicComments.name: Patricia Renderos
publicComments.summary: Advocated (in Spanish, interpreted) for retaining TK and kinder programs at Hoover, emphasizing the importance of teacher stability and requesting coaching support for new teachers.

publicComments.name: Agustin Espino
publicComments.summary: Asked the board to protect mental health and TK programs and to ensure that any budget cuts are made equitably, particularly for the Latino community, given the disparity in outcomes between schools like Hoover and Orion.

publicComments.name: Lizbeth Estrada
publicComments.summary: Spoke (in Spanish, interpreted) as a Hoover parent expressing deep concern about cuts to teacher retention and MTSS intervention specialists, arguing that reducing reading and math specialists creates long-term educational debt.

publicComments.name: Cristian Vasquez
publicComments.summary: Shared (in Spanish, interpreted) a personal testimony about the difference in resources between his older and younger children's educations at Clifford, advocating for preserving staff and specialist positions.

publicComments.name: Rita Melton

publicComments.name: Dora Ramirez

publicComments.name: Noah Gaspar
publicComments.summary: As a parent of an incoming TK student at Orion, he expressed concern about the lack of on-campus aftercare options for TK-age children and asked the board to help find solutions.

publicComments.name: Hai-Ching Yang
publicComments.summary: As a parent of an incoming TK student at Orion, she echoed concerns about insufficient aftercare options for working families and urged the board to open additional classrooms and extend aftercare eligibility to children 4 years and older.

publicComments.name: Michelle Stroebe
publicComments.summary: Thanked Dr. Baker for the update on aftercare challenges, expressed appreciation for the board's efforts, and offered parent support in addressing the TK aftercare situation.

items.title: Discussion of Citizens' Initiative and District-Sponsored Parcel Tax

publicComments.name: Carlo Contavalli
publicComments.summary: As a parent and proponent of the citizens' initiative parcel tax, he described the district's funding challenges and expressed support for a square-footage-based parcel tax as an urgent priority.

publicComments.name: Jessica Shade
publicComments.summary: As a leader of the citizens' initiative effort, she expressed support for any form of parcel tax and stated that if the district poll does not show sufficient support for a district-sponsored measure, she will continue pursuing the citizens' initiative.

publicComments.name: Adina Olsen
publicComments.summary: As a parent working on the citizens' initiative, she echoed support for additional school funding through either a district-sponsored or citizens' initiative parcel tax.

publicComments.name: Maria Stockton
publicComments.summary: Introduced herself as the new CSEA Chapter President and thanked her negotiating team and district representatives for productive classified employee negotiations.

publicComments.name: Diana Escalante Ocon
publicComments.summary: As a 14-year librarian at Adelante Selby, she explained in detail how every minute of her schedule is used and argued that reducing her hours from 37.5 to 30 per week would significantly impair essential library maintenance and student services.

publicComments.name: Devon Mullane
publicComments.summary: An 8th grade teacher at Hoover, he expressed appreciation for Hoover administration, advocated for support staff district-wide, and specifically praised the librarians by name for their dedication to students.

publicComments.name: Meghan O'Reilly Green
publicComments.summary: A parent in the Orion Mandarin Immersion program, she expressed support for placing a second Co-op TK class in the second enrollment round and called for community-wide discussion about growth at Orion given that projections had underestimated enrollment.

publicComments.name: Carl Landers
publicComments.summary: Supported portable AC units and HVAC upgrades as an interim solution but urged the board not to use bond funds for short-term rentals, suggesting rentals be paid from general funds while bond money covers capital purchases.

publicComments.name: Devon Mullane
publicComments.summary: Thanked the board for the AC solution but raised concern about portable floor units taking up classroom space in already-crowded classrooms, suggesting roof-mounted permanent units would be preferable.

publicComments.name: Laura Lentz

publicComments.name: Lisbeth Estrada

publicComments.name: Julio Suchite
publicComments.summary: Spoke in Spanish (interpreted) expressing deep gratitude to the board and community, emphasizing trust in the teachers and the transformative impact Connect has had on his family.

publicComments.name: Amanda Vaughn
publicComments.summary: Spoke as Connect's art and newcomer teacher, praising the school's implementation of systematic ELD for newcomer students and the quality of the school's art program.

publicComments.name: Meda Okelo
publicComments.summary: Described joining Connect as a long-term guest teacher, praised its SEL and project-based learning focus, and strongly urged the board to renew the charter.

publicComments.name: Anton Estaniel
publicComments.summary: Spoke as Connect's music teacher, describing the school-wide musical production 'Space is the Place' as a powerful example of project-based learning involving nearly every student.

publicComments.name: Walter Soto Franco
publicComments.summary: A recent Connect graduate originally from Guatemala shared how the school helped him learn English and develop his skills, expressing deep gratitude to the staff.

publicComments.name: Itzamar Giron
publicComments.summary: A Connect alumna now studying mechanical engineering at San Francisco State shared how the school made a lasting positive impact on her life and spoke fondly of her teachers and the school community.

publicComments.name: Ashley Solis
publicComments.summary: An eighth-grade current Connect student shared that the school helped her learn to speak and read in English and that the staff treats students well.

items.title: Call to Order
Roll Call
Welcome by the School Board President, Mike Wells
Changes to the Agenda
Approval of Agenda
Oral Communication

publicComments.name: Meng Zhou
publicComments.summary: Advocated for expanding Mandarin Immersion TK/K enrollment to accommodate all first-round applicants, presenting data on growing demand, waitlists, and academic benefits of the MI program.

publicComments.name: Max Zeiberg
publicComments.summary: Ceded his speaking time to Meng Zhou to allow an extended presentation on behalf of MI families.

publicComments.name: Michelle Stroebe
publicComments.summary: Presented data showing MI programs produce strong academic outcomes especially for socioeconomically disadvantaged students, and formally requested the board add a TK/K classroom and consider a supplemental application process.

publicComments.name: Kimberly Abbi
publicComments.summary: As a current MI TK parent from a diverse background, praised the program's tight-knit community and its ability to give her son fluency in Mandarin, and urged the board to expand it.

publicComments.name: Weije
publicComments.summary: As a prospective Chinese immigrant family and Redwood City homeowner, expressed concern about limited K spots and requested program expansion so their daughter could maintain Mandarin heritage language.

publicComments.name: Christina Lee
publicComments.summary: Described being stuck at number one on the MI waitlist for over a year with a summer-birthday kindergartner, and urged the board to expand the program so all interested students can access it.

publicComments.name: Saoirse Rafferty
publicComments.summary: A current fifth-grade MI student shared her personal journey from struggling in first grade to placing 17th nationally in a math competition, crediting the rigor of Mandarin immersion, and urged the board to support and sustain the program.

publicComments.name: Meghan O'Reilly-Green
publicComments.summary: Ceded most of her time to Meng Zhou for additional data presentation on MI growth projections, emphasizing the program's value for mixed-heritage and non-native families and requesting the board accommodate first-round applicants.

publicComments.name: Chris Lao
publicComments.summary: Shared that his family moved to Redwood City hoping their daughter could eventually attend the MI program, and thanked the board for considering expanding it to support Asian heritage connection.

publicComments.name: Catherine Stuart
publicComments.summary: As an NSA parent whose child previously attended MI, expressed support for expanding both MI and North Star programs given their strong waitlists and academic results, and urged continued investment in both.

publicComments.name: Xingxing Liu
publicComments.summary: As a first-generation Chinese immigrant and Redwood City resident since 2019, expressed hope to enroll her two children in the MI program to preserve their Mandarin heritage language.

publicComments.name: James Herbold
publicComments.summary: As a prospective MI parent whose wife is from China, echoed other speakers' support for expanding the program so his daughter Chloe can learn the family's language at a school just a block from their home.

2024-12-17-board-meeting.agendaChanges: Per the minutes and transcript, the Reception (Section 5) was moved to occur before the Annual Board Officer's Rotation (Section 4). The oath of office (Section 3) occurred before the reception, and the Annual Board Officer's Rotation occurred after the reception. This resequencing was noted under Changes to the Agenda (Section 6). The actual order was: Call to Order, Roll Call, Welcome, Oath of Office, Reception, Annual Board Officer's Rotation, Changes to the Agenda, Approval of Agenda, then the remaining items in order.

items.title: Call to Order
Roll Call
Welcome by the School Board President

publicComments.name: Dennis McBride
publicComments.summary: Thanked Janet Lawson for her nine years of dedicated service, recounting her integrity, calm leadership through crises including school closures and COVID, and her personal friendship.

publicComments.name: Mario Rendon
publicComments.summary: Representing Congressman Kevin Mullin's office, commended Janet Lawson for her perseverance and public service, presenting a token of appreciation.

publicComments.name: Joan Dentler
publicComments.summary: Representing State Senator Josh Becker and Assemblymembers Diane Papan and Marc Berman, presented Janet Lawson with a legislative resolution commending her dedication and service as a board trustee, and also acknowledged Elisa McAvoy's contributions.

publicComments.name: Lori McBride
publicComments.summary: Praised Elisa McAvoy's extensive volunteerism, community involvement, professional background, and 17 years of tireless service to the Redwood City School District and beyond.

publicComments.name: Mario Rendon
publicComments.summary: Representing Congressman Kevin Mullin, presented Elisa McAvoy with a Congressional Record entry in her honor, thanking her for 17 years of service and her invaluable guidance on education issues.

publicComments.name: Dennis McBride
publicComments.summary: Delivered an extended tribute to Elisa McAvoy, highlighting her 17 years of board service, her work on parcel taxes and bonds, her book distribution efforts, her advocacy, and her personal qualities of positivity and dedication to students.

publicComments.name: Marissa McAvoy
publicComments.summary: Elisa McAvoy's daughter shared pride in her mother's work, noting that Elisa actually increased her commitment after her daughters left the district.

publicComments.name: Meghan O'Reilly-Green
publicComments.summary: Expressed appreciation for the board's difficult work and advocated for a larger parcel tax, comparing RCSD's current $85 parcel tax unfavorably to neighboring districts.

publicComments.name: Mary Ann Nakhla
publicComments.summary: Urged the board to reconsider cutting mental health liaison positions, emphasizing the deep relationships liaisons have built with students across the district.

publicComments.name: Lisbeth Estrada
publicComments.summary: Expressed deep appreciation to Dr. Baker for prioritizing equity and supporting the Hoover community, including special education students.

publicComments.name: Saoirse Rafferty
publicComments.summary: A fourth-grade student at Orion who expressed love for her school and teachers and hoped a larger parcel tax could be passed so all kids have what they need.

publicComments.name: Sue Jorgensen
publicComments.summary: Taft librarian who criticized inadequate communication from the district about librarian layoffs, expressed distrust, and declined to support a parcel tax at this time.

publicComments.name: Ann Berljafa
publicComments.summary: Advocated for enshrining a 25-to-1 class size ratio for K-2 classrooms in the collective bargaining agreement, warning that larger classes cause students to fall through the cracks.

publicComments.name: Shari Tsuchiyama
publicComments.summary: First-grade teacher at Roosevelt who shared personal experience of the benefits of smaller class sizes and urged the board to permanently cap TK-2nd grade at 25 students.

publicComments.name: Jessica McLain
publicComments.summary: Education specialist who raised safety and quality concerns about increasing caseloads in moderate-to-severe special education classrooms, calling for an additional ESLC and SLC class.

publicComments.name: Alison Sudol
publicComments.summary: Teacher of an extensive support needs classroom who described increasing unsafe behaviors correlated with rising class sizes and proposed opening one additional ESN and SLC classroom.

publicComments.name: Erinn Washburn
publicComments.summary: Music teacher who outlined the academic, social-emotional, and developmental benefits of well-funded music education programs and urged administrators and parents to prioritize them.

publicComments.name: Dianne Gutierrez
publicComments.summary: School counselor at Orion who shared a story about a dysregulated student to illustrate the importance of having a counselor available as a safety net for students.

publicComments.name: Lilli Milton
publicComments.summary: Orion and Kennedy parent and RCEF board member who urged the board to reject the resolution, seek authentic educator input, and pursue an equitable approach to cuts while rebuilding community trust.

publicComments.name: Dave Privasch
publicComments.summary: Expressed that cutting librarians, mental health staff, and STEM teachers seems unconscionable given the area's wealth, and echoed calls for creative solutions before approving reductions.

items.title: Adoption of Resolution No. 8, Reduction of Certain Classified Services for Fiscal Year 2023-2024

publicComments.name: Salvador Gomez Nava
publicComments.summary: Spanish-speaking parent of a Taft student who emphasized the critical importance of school-based counseling for children dealing with trauma and urged the board to maintain those services.

publicComments.name: Ana Solorio
publicComments.summary: President of CSEA Chapter 5 who opposed the layoff of any classified employees and requested the board not approve the resolution.

publicComments.name: Connie Hamilton
publicComments.summary: LMA librarian at Clifford School who questioned the equity of laying off three experienced, lower-paid librarians while planning to hire new LMTA staff at higher pay, and expressed devastation at losing her position.

publicComments.name: Beverly Aguilar
publicComments.summary: LMA librarian at Garfield School who, together with Connie Hamilton, asked the board not to eliminate their positions, citing 30 combined years of experience, bilingual skills, and the reclassification process already underway.

items.title: Call to Order / Roll Call
Oral Communication on Study Session Items Only
Study Session – Discussion on Dyslexia

publicComments.name: Ors Neighbor
publicComments.summary: A father described his son Leor's experience being diagnosed with dyslexia privately at great cost after the school determined he did not have a specific learning disability, and urged the community to find ways to teach reading to all children regardless of resources.

publicComments.name: Giselle Hale
publicComments.summary: A parent of two dyslexic students and a community advocate urged the board to treat dyslexia as a general education issue, highlighted districts making gains by training all teachers in Orton-Gillingham techniques, and emphasized that implementation fidelity matters as much as curriculum selection.

publicComments.name: Fallon Scoggins
publicComments.summary: A mother of a severely dyslexic and dysgraphic third-grade son described his struggles in school, the inadequacy of pullout services and after-school interventions, the disproportionate impact on Black students, and urged the board to make structured literacy instruction available to all students throughout the school day.

publicComments.name: Jennifer Salma
publicComments.summary: A regional leader with Decoding Dyslexia California thanked the board for engaging with the California Dyslexia Guidelines, emphasized that dyslexia is a neurological difference with many strengths, called for structured literacy programs to be implemented with fidelity and for principals to support teachers in implementation, and noted that most dyslexic students should be served in general education with tier 1 and tier 2 support.

publicComments.name: Ors Neighbor
publicComments.summary: A father described his son Leor's experience being diagnosed with dyslexia privately at significant expense after the school determined he did not qualify for special education services despite low reading scores, and urged the district to support all students regardless of financial means.

publicComments.name: Giselle Hale
publicComments.summary: A parent of two dyslexic students and community advocate urged the board to treat dyslexia as a general education issue, highlighted districts succeeding through Orton-Gillingham training for all teachers, and stressed that implementation fidelity matters more than curriculum choice alone.

publicComments.name: Fallon Scoggins
publicComments.summary: A mother of a son with severe dyslexia and dysgraphia described the emotional and academic toll on her family, the inaccessibility of after-school interventions for many families, and urgently called for structured literacy instruction to be available throughout the school day for all students.

publicComments.name: Jennifer Salma
publicComments.summary: A regional leader with Decoding Dyslexia California thanked the district for engaging with the California Dyslexia Guidelines, emphasized dyslexia as a neurological difference with associated strengths rather than a disability, and urged fidelity in program implementation and robust principal and teacher support.

Update on Workforce Housing
Oral Communication

publicComments.name: Chris R.
publicComments.summary: Argued that using Prop 39 bond funds for workforce housing violates the California Constitution, questioned whether teacher housing actually serves teachers effectively, and suggested parcel taxes or housing vouchers as better alternatives.

publicComments.name: Elizabeth Starks
publicComments.summary: Expressed support for teachers but emphasized that children and families — especially in the Spanish-speaking community — need more after-school spaces and resources, and urged the district to focus on hiring dedicated teachers.

publicComments.name: Maria Stockton
publicComments.summary: Speaking as a classified union leader, she defended the essential role of classified staff, shared that her own rent had risen far beyond any district raise, and expressed strong support for workforce housing as a relief for dedicated long-term employees.

publicComments.name: Lupe Guzman
publicComments.summary: As principal of Hoover Community School, she emphasized that all school staff are vital, described how staff cannot afford to live locally, and expressed support for the housing project as a way to rebuild community and reduce staff stress.

publicComments.name: Yolanda Richardson
publicComments.summary: As a South San Francisco school board visitor, she asked about the quality and durability of the Jefferson housing construction and how rent revenues are handled relative to the bond and general fund.

publicComments.name: Janet Coches
publicComments.summary: As a parent, she shared that her child lost a beloved fifth-grade teacher who left due to rent affordability, described ongoing transportation challenges for students, and asked for support in keeping teachers in the community.

publicComments.name: Erin Washburn
publicComments.summary: As president of the Redwood City Teachers Association, she confirmed that teachers genuinely want this program, shared personal examples of teachers burdened by housing costs, and expressed strong support for moving the project forward.

items.title: Call to Order / Roll Call
Report out on Closed Session from January 20, 2023
Welcome by the Board President
Changes to the Agenda
Approval of Agenda
Oral Communication

publicComments.name: Peter Cohen
publicComments.summary: Thanked district staff for correcting enrollment issues with school-of-choice programs, and advocated for expanding Mandarin Immersion TK programs to align with California's 2030 bilingual goal and upcoming Universal TK expansion.

publicComments.name: Alexis Betts
publicComments.summary: Advocated for expanding MI enrollment to accommodate all interested families, noting the current interest level represents an opportunity for a sustainable, healthy program through 8th grade.

publicComments.name: Megan O'Reilly
publicComments.summary: Advocated for underserved families in the MI program who need more support, requested a different structural approach to serve both program goals, and urged the district to apply for the TCLP program.

publicComments.name: William Dennis
publicComments.summary: Expressed strong support for sibling priority in MI enrollment so his younger daughter can attend the same school as his son.

publicComments.name: Deepika Germany (spouse speaking)
publicComments.summary: Advocated for an additional Mandarin Immersion TK class, stating their daughter is currently on the waitlist and an extra year in the program would be very beneficial.

publicComments.name: Tina Chung
publicComments.summary: Advocated for additional Mandarin Immersion TK classes to benefit more students in the community, including her son who has some Mandarin background.

publicComments.name: Ronnie Le
publicComments.summary: Advocated for expanding the Mandarin Immersion program, citing its academic rigor comparable to private immersion schools and the cognitive benefits of bilingual education for her incoming kindergartener.

publicComments.name: Jackie Chen
publicComments.summary: Shared the emotional difficulty of the MI waitlist experience and urged the board to create two TK MI classes and three kindergarten MI classes.

publicComments.name: Julia Selesnick
publicComments.summary: Praised the MI program's academic rigor and advocated for expanding both the kindergarten and TK programs to make this high-quality public school option accessible to more children.

publicComments.name: Alex (last name not given)
publicComments.summary: Advocated for expanding the Mandarin Immersion program at Orion, drawing on personal experience growing up bilingual and emphasizing the broad human and community benefits of early language immersion.

publicComments.name: Angela Kang
publicComments.summary: Advocated for additional MI TK classes so that all families who want Mandarin Immersion education for their children, including herself with a waitlisted child, can have the opportunity.

publicComments.name: Sam Christensen
publicComments.summary: Stated that the MI program is the only program his family is interested in, otherwise they will consider private school, and expressed support for expanding TK Mandarin Immersion.

publicComments.name: Chris Terry
publicComments.summary: Raised concerns about fairness in the MI lottery, particularly the Odyssey Preschool preference (costing $1,700/month) and the TK preference potentially crowding out other kindergarten applicants, and asked for lottery reform or more classes.

publicComments.name: Jasper Chen
publicComments.summary: Expressed gratitude for the community's transformation and urged the board to invest boldly in expanding the MI program, leveraging the new campus to build a larger, thriving community.

publicComments.name: Anna Lee Moraz
publicComments.summary: Spoke in Spanish to advocate for more support and accessibility for low-income Latino families in the Mandarin Immersion program, including concerns about the cost of after-school programs.

publicComments.name: Keith Simmons
publicComments.summary: Asked the board to extend sibling priority to TK, expand MI to two TK classes and three kindergarten classes, and consider expanding Adelante as well given the importance of early language acquisition.

publicComments.name: Heather Stamper
publicComments.summary: Supported expanding TK and K MI classrooms, noting the program shrank from two kindergarten classes during COVID and expressing concern about the snowball effect of attrition on the program's long-term viability.

publicComments.name: Mele Rosallo
publicComments.summary: Advocated for more funding and support for MI students with learning disabilities, including a Mandarin resource teacher and aides for math, and an additional TK class.

publicComments.name: Richard Pont
publicComments.summary: Shared his family's positive experience with the MI program and urged expansion so more families, including those with IEP students, can join and thrive.

publicComments.name: Micah Bonara
publicComments.summary: Reported his twin boys got off the waitlist and into kindergarten, and called for expansion of both TK and K MI to spare other families the emotional stress of the waitlist process.

publicComments.name: Sarah Rutherford
publicComments.summary: Asked for consistency in the sibling priority policy between kindergarten and TK, especially given the upcoming rollout of Universal TK.

publicComments.name: Tiffany Chao Bev
publicComments.summary: Shared that her family moved to Redwood City specifically for the MI program and urged the board to expand kindergarten, TK, and the elementary program, as they would consider relocating if their son cannot get in.

publicComments.name: Xiang Yen
publicComments.summary: Asked what parents and the community can do to help advance expansion of the MI program, and requested guidance from the board on next steps.

publicComments.name: Julia Feng
publicComments.summary: Questioned whether it makes sense to artificially cap demand for MI in a district with declining enrollment, and emphasized that TK/Kinder are the only entry windows for immersion programs.

publicComments.name: Doshi (last name not given)
publicComments.summary: Advocated for an additional MI class, noting that the preschool and sibling preferences effectively eliminated her single-child family's chances despite being a district resident and public school advocate.

publicComments.name: Philip Guy
publicComments.summary: Urged the board to expand the MI program in every way possible and asked the community to be told how they can help make that happen.

publicComments.name: Olanes Villanueva (interpreted)
publicComments.summary: Advocated on behalf of Latino families in the MI program for more support, including in-school tutoring services, so that their children are not invisible and can reach grade level and succeed in the program.

publicComments.name: Iliana Harrison
publicComments.summary: Thanked Dr. Baker for supporting the MI program at the Lunar New Year fair and asked the board for two TK classes and three kindergarten classes, offering parent community support to make it happen.

publicComments.name: Kyle Zipes
publicComments.summary: Argued that deciding a child's education by lottery is unacceptable and urged the board to accommodate all families who want to attend MI, including investigating a dedicated campus if needed.

publicComments.name: Chris Robel
publicComments.summary: Opposed the bond measure, arguing it was a bad time for new taxes, that a parcel tax would be more equitable and flexible, and raising concerns about enrollment decline, interest costs to taxpayers, and the measure's inability to fund teachers or academic programs.

publicComments.name: Katerina Schwaidenbeck
publicComments.summary: Supported the bond, describing poor bathroom and kitchen facilities at her daughter's school and how a central kitchen could improve the quality and nutritional value of school meals.

publicComments.name: Jessica Shade
publicComments.summary: Supported the bond, citing the need for updated health, safety, and educational facilities, the potential for a central kitchen for healthier meals, and praising the district's track record of completing Measure T on time and under budget.

publicComments.name: Jessica Meunier
publicComments.summary: Supported the bond, expressing excitement about the central kitchen potential for improved nutrition, updated safety standards, and the district's track record of fiscal responsibility.

publicComments.name: Elmer Martinez
publicComments.summary: Supported the bond as a community volunteer, emphasizing the need for critical facility upgrades at aging schools to meet health, safety, and educational standards and strengthen community property values.

publicComments.name: Ileana Harrison
publicComments.summary: Supported the bond as a pediatrician and parent, praising the new Orion building, noting broad parent support, and advocating especially for a central kitchen to enable cooking fresh meals for students.

publicComments.name: Jessica Shade
publicComments.summary: Voiced support for the new school bond, called for central kitchen language to be included, and praised the district's efforts.

publicComments.name: Katerina Schoenbach

items.title: Recommended Options to the School Board Regarding North Star Academy (NSA)

publicComments.name: Matt Schwal
publicComments.summary: Argued the NSA Forward Committee was negatively framed and urged the board to reframe the discussion positively, celebrating North Star's success rather than proposing changes.

publicComments.name: Cameron Hoffman
publicComments.summary: Argued the proposals are being rushed and lack evidence, compared North Star favorably to U46 in Illinois, and proposed a district-wide talent development program called 'SHINE' to serve younger learners rather than restructuring North Star.

publicComments.name: Tony Webel
publicComments.summary: Shared his experience as a committee member and parent of students at both Roosevelt and North Star, and argued that board bylaws require keeping student learning as the primary focus, finding no data justifying grade configuration or location changes.

publicComments.name: Diana Fu
publicComments.summary: Drew on her experience helping start the MI program to argue the district should provide more choices and improve other schools rather than restricting a choice program like North Star.

publicComments.name: Allie Gilmore
publicComments.summary: Speaker was called but did not respond or unmute.

publicComments.name: Wenjing Sheng
publicComments.summary: Speaker was called but was unable to unmute and did not provide comment.

publicComments.name: Lone Hin
publicComments.summary: Described her first-grader's unmet academic needs at their neighborhood school and argued that scaling back North Star would leave more students without appropriate challenge.

publicComments.name: Megan O'Neill
publicComments.summary: Argued that options A through C would serve fewer students and contradict the stated goal of serving more students more effectively, and that neighborhood schools must first be ready to serve gifted learners before any North Star grades are cut.

publicComments.name: Matt Collier
publicComments.summary: As a parent of young children and former magnet school student himself, urged the board to preserve K–8 North Star as a standalone school and to consider Ms. Hoffman's alternative proposal.

publicComments.name: Anthony Low
publicComments.summary: Stated that three of the four options effectively close half of North Star, called all options deficient, and said his family would likely leave the district if a 6–8 option were chosen.

publicComments.name: Andrew Hoffman
publicComments.summary: Argued the recommendations target North Star rather than addressing underperforming schools, warned the options would negatively impact North Star's mission, and urged the board not to adopt incomplete proposals.

publicComments.name: Heidi Wall
publicComments.summary: Criticized the proposals as short-sighted and premature, cited district-wide problems like declining test scores and teacher retention, and called for using North Star's proven methods to improve other schools rather than dismantling it.

publicComments.name: Susan Bouchard
publicComments.summary: Argued that capping the number of qualified students allowed to transfer to NSA from neighborhood schools would increase the perception of elitism and create more divisiveness, and asked the board to reject and rework all proposals.

publicComments.name: Costas Dimitropoulos
publicComments.summary: Asked for empirical evidence supporting any proposed changes and questioned whether there is data proving that altering a high-achieving school like North Star would improve outcomes at lower-performing schools.

publicComments.name: Kelly McCarthy
publicComments.summary: Questioned whether the community pulse had been taken since COVID, called the effort a waste of resources given widespread learning loss, and urged the board to instead use resources to improve neighborhood schools.

publicComments.name: Casey Wright
publicComments.summary: Called several options 'casually cruel,' warned the board against falling for the sunk cost fallacy, and implored them to postpone a decision and demand better-researched options.

publicComments.name: Catherine Stewart
publicComments.summary: Observed that virtually every prior speaker opposed changing or dismantling North Star and argued that the general sentiment—'if it ain't broke, don't fix it'—should guide the board.

publicComments.name: Gautam Dutta
publicComments.summary: As a local attorney, Asian American civic leader, and North Star parent, echoed 'if it ain't broke, don't fix it,' and asked the board to reject all four options and instead improve support and resources for all schools.

publicComments.name: Jing Zhu
publicComments.summary: Warned that the proposed changes would create many unhappy parents who become reliable voters against the board, and urged the board to think carefully about the disruption these options would cause to North Star families' daily lives.

publicComments.name: Kirsten McMullen
publicComments.summary: Called the recommendations insufficient and lacking an evidence-based approach, noting they mainly solve the third-grade attrition issue while creating new problems, and urged deeper research and cost-benefit analysis.

publicComments.name: Bara Altaba
publicComments.summary: As a parent of current NSA students who transferred from private school because of North Star, urged the board not to break a nationally recognized school and to instead bring other schools up to North Star's level.

publicComments.name: Michelle Fogarty
publicComments.summary: Argued the proposals take direct aim at one successful school without solving district-wide shortcomings, criticized use of outdated data from 2018, and urged the board to conduct new research before implementing any changes.

publicComments.name: Katie Goetz
publicComments.summary: As a North Star Forward Committee member, encouraged the board to consider and move forward with the proposal to make North Star a 6–8 school on the Bayside, stating it best addresses the committee's three charges.

publicComments.name: Christopher Bouchard
publicComments.summary: Asked the board to reject all four proposals and pause major changes, citing a flawed and biased committee process built on four-year-old data, and called for a reset with current community input.

publicComments.name: David Wright
publicComments.summary: Implored the board to defer the decision and request a revised set of options aligned to the committee's stated goals, warning against committing to a process not in students' best interests.

publicComments.name: Mike Beebe
publicComments.summary: Summarized three broadly supported elements from Dr. Baker's plans (outreach, test prep access, logistics like transportation), suggested spreading entrance grades across third through fifth to reduce the third-grade attrition pinch, and emphasized district-wide learning loss as the true priority.

publicComments.name: Meredith Park
publicComments.summary: Raised traffic concerns near North Star, warned against adding K–2 at the Duane location, and suggested exploring a neighborhood boundary middle school with an IB program on Duane as an alternative approach.

publicComments.name: Nathan Schmidt
publicComments.summary: Using a 'ten boats' analogy, argued the board's duty is to save the underperforming schools rather than compromising the one that is succeeding.

publicComments.name: Mohamed Tabrizi
publicComments.summary: Ceded time to Cameron Hoffman and added that spending district resources on this process instead of lower-income students' education is unacceptable, urging trustees to reject all options and use scientific data.

publicComments.name: Lily Melton
publicComments.summary: As an Orion parent, urged the board to move forward with bold structural changes and noted that a large community of parents who want district-wide improvement supports significant change even if not loudly represented tonight.

publicComments.name: Beatrice Chan
publicComments.summary: Called the options deeply flawed, asked the board to instead focus on introducing enrichment programs in community schools and helping current students meet grade-level expectations rather than dismantling North Star.

publicComments.name: Maribel Ng
publicComments.summary: Shared her child's struggles before finding North Star, emphasized that students who go there have often struggled elsewhere and need an environment that understands them, and suggested spreading that philosophy across all Redwood City schools.

items.title: Call to Order / Roll Call
Welcome by the School Board President
Changes to the Agenda
Approval of Agenda
Oral Communication
Review of Preliminary Ideas Generated by North Star Forward Committee

publicComments.name: David Weekley
publicComments.summary: Urged the board to center students and maximize every student's potential, changing North Star if it harms non-North Star students.

publicComments.name: Lily Milton
publicComments.summary: Representing committee members, she thanked the board for the process and recommended changing grade configuration and bringing compacted/enrichment curriculum to all schools, while urging urgent action on equity and broader structural issues.

publicComments.name: Unidentified speaker
publicComments.summary: Argued that Bayside access data is misleading since many Bayside families already access North Star via choice/charter, that North Star is not broken and serves its students well at no extra cost, and urged focus on the 70% of students behind academically rather than altering a well-functioning program.

publicComments.name: John Kinsel
publicComments.summary: Expressed strong support for North Star as a program that keeps students challenged and engaged, and suggested the focus should be on improving other schools rather than changing North Star.

publicComments.name: Owen Webel
publicComments.summary: As a North Star graduate, described how he was disengaged at his previous school and thrived at North Star where he found peers at his level and learned more effectively.

publicComments.name: Tony Webel
publicComments.summary: As a parent of multiple children in the district including one at North Star and one at Roosevelt, he emphasized that the primary objective should be the best equitable learning environment for all students, and flagged concerns about the Jamboard document as potentially misleading.

publicComments.name: Jessica Nodi
publicComments.summary: As a Roy Cloud parent and educator, she expressed support for starting North Star at kindergarten, bringing enrichment programming to all schools, and offering enrollment by equal percentages from each school.

publicComments.name: Katie Morello
publicComments.summary: As a Henry Ford parent, she expressed concern about the emotional impact on children losing friends when classmates leave for North Star in third grade and suggested middle school might be a better entry point.

publicComments.name: Kaylee Vega
publicComments.summary: As a Henry Ford parent, she said her family chose the district largely because of North Star access, expressed concern about unmet academic needs at neighborhood schools, and urged the board to improve other schools rather than dismantle a highly ranked program.

publicComments.name: Loan Hin
publicComments.summary: As a Henry Ford parent and former magnet school student herself, she said her family may leave the district if North Star is not available at third grade, and argued that magnet programs like North Star provide economic mobility through education and that equity issues can be addressed without eliminating the format.

publicComments.name: Elle Kolekar
publicComments.summary: As a Bayside family with children at both Hillside and North Star schools, she argued that inequity is a deeper district-wide issue that North Star alone did not create, and called on the board to invest in vibrant, diverse Bayside community schools.

publicComments.name: Alice Harkey
publicComments.summary: As North Star's parent club president, she credited North Star with transforming her children's education, supported equal access to testing and more Bayside outreach, busing, and after-school support, and asked the board to preserve the curriculum and enrichment opportunities.

publicComments.name: Shonda Stewart
publicComments.summary: As a parent with children at both North Star and Orion, she raised concerns about North Star's fundraising concentrating resources inequitably, the school's culture being unwelcoming to lower-income families, and the limited Bayside outreach compared to Mandarin immersion community outreach.

publicComments.name: Katie Goetz
publicComments.summary: As a North Star Forward Committee member, she argued that the district has an obligation to make enriched programming available to all students, not just those identified through testing as gifted, and thanked the board for its work toward greater equity.

publicComments.name: Nicole Kowaki
publicComments.summary: Speaking on behalf of 20 co-signing district parents, she cited stark demographic disparities between North Star and the broader district and called publicly funded North Star as currently structured unethical, urging the board to support the equitable solutions proposed by the committee.

publicComments.name: Daryl Norcott
publicComments.summary: As a Clifford parent, he argued that North Star reinforces achievement gaps through biased standardized testing, racially segregates school communities, makes children who don't qualify feel defective, and divides communities, and urged bringing enrichment methods to students rather than concentrating them in one school.

publicComments.name: Heidi von Brielle
publicComments.summary: She raised environmental concerns about car trips to North Star, argued that all Redwood City schools share the same finish line, and called for implementing options that eliminate the 'musical chairs' caused by third-grade migration.

publicComments.name: Mike Beebe
publicComments.summary: As a North Star third-grade parent, he urged careful evaluation of whether the issue is admissions process design or information and logistics barriers before switching to a hard percentage admit model, and suggested expanded information and shuttle service for Bayside families.

publicComments.name: Ann Hinesek
publicComments.summary: As a North Star Forward Committee member, she highlighted bringing the Renzulli method to all schools and equalizing PTO/PTA funding distribution as key equity ideas, and supported changing grade configuration, hiring Spanish-speaking staff, capping per-school admittance, and possibly moving North Star to the Bayside.

publicComments.summary: Read a letter on behalf of the district technology team describing how classified tech staff were required to work on-site throughout the pandemic without receiving the same financial compensation as teachers who were later incentivized to return.

publicComments.summary: Followed up on the letter, questioning why the tech department and other classified staff who worked on-site the entire pandemic were not offered the same financial incentives as teachers, and expressing disappointment that the proposed MOU compensation of five restricted holidays was not comparable to the teacher stipend.

publicComments.name: Maria Stockton
publicComments.summary: A 43-year district employee described a decline in respect for classified staff, noted inequitable treatment compared to certificated staff during COVID, and invited board members to visit schools and speak directly with classified employees.

publicComments.name: Ana Solorio
publicComments.summary: Presented a letter with signatures from classified members requesting board support in MOU negotiations, emphasizing that classified workers who were on-site during the pandemic deserve compensation comparable to the teacher stipends, not just five holiday days.

publicComments.name: Johanna Rasmussen
publicComments.summary: Described her fourth-grade son being assaulted for the third time at Roosevelt School despite a safety plan in place, and asked the board to intervene and address escalating bullying and violence on the school's playground.

Second Reading and Approval of Board Policy 5116.1 - Intradistrict Open Enrollment

publicComments.name: David Weekley
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy, arguing that restricting intradistrict transfers hurts students and the district by limiting parental choice, and that the way to protect programs like Mandarin Immersion is to invest in their quality rather than lock families in.

publicComments.name: Eric Liu
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy as an Asian American parent of a Mandarin Immersion student, stating that barring choice program students from applying to North Star felt discriminatory and contrary to Redwood City's values of diversity and equality.

publicComments.name: Connie Kwan
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy, describing the unfair dilemma it creates for parents who must choose between staying in a choice program or leaving early to preserve eligibility for North Star, and asked the committee to consider alternative solutions to attrition.

publicComments.name: Peter Cowan
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy, noting the difficult bind it creates for families with children in different programs, criticized the timing during enrollment season, argued the policy is a band-aid on a third-grade attrition cliff, and called for transparency instead of 'unspoken rules.'

publicComments.name: Dennis Lee
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy as a parent of a rising kindergartner accepted to Mandarin Immersion, arguing it raises the stakes of choosing a school by requiring parents to predict their child's needs years in advance and that it unfairly limits future choices while potentially benefiting private school families.

publicComments.name: Michelle Hsu
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy, arguing that North Star and Mandarin Immersion are not competing on fair ground given North Star's third-grade entry and testing-based admissions, and urged the committee to address equity issues in North Star's admissions process instead.

publicComments.name: Jennifer Barnett
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy as a Mandarin Immersion kindergarten parent, calling it shortsighted and inequitable, arguing it will not fix the exodus to North Star and instead should prompt examination of North Star's own problematic policies.

publicComments.name: Diana Chang
publicComments.summary: Submitted a written comment opposing the restriction on transferring between non-boundary schools, calling it unnecessarily limiting for families who discover a choice school is not the right fit, and questioning whether there are better ways to distribute opportunities equitably.

publicComments.name: Alvin Cheon
publicComments.summary: As an Orion Parent Participation parent, expressed concern that removing intradistrict transfer incentives would push families away from public school, and suggested alternatives such as embedding advanced coursework at the classroom level within each school rather than concentrating it at North Star.

publicComments.name: Eric Garcia
publicComments.summary: Submitted a written comment opposing the policy, arguing it removes parents' ability to choose what is best for their children's evolving educational needs and that schools losing students to transfers should instead be empowered to improve their programs.

publicComments.name: Christina Jade Lee Simmons
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy and called for deeper structural reform, arguing that North Star perpetuates inequality as the least economically integrated school in the district, and encouraged the board to consider starting North Star in sixth grade and investing in enrichment at every elementary school.

publicComments.name: Kyle Zeibes
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy as a Mandarin Immersion kindergarten parent and budget committee member, arguing it will harm equity by trapping low-income families and push higher-income families to private school, and suggested delaying North Star until sixth grade as a preferable alternative.

publicComments.name: Brian Hicks
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy, challenging the administration's framing of it as formalizing an 'unwritten rule' since the actual long-standing practice has been that families can move between choice programs, and requested that any future policy not be applied retroactively to families already enrolled.

publicComments.name: Elle Kolekar
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy as an Orion Parent Participation parent, warning it will cause families interested in North Star to attend private school for K–2 and could devastate Orion's Parent Participation program, and instead urged the district to invest in making neighborhood schools more attractive.

publicComments.name: Keith Simmons
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy as a Mandarin Immersion parent, echoing others' concerns and specifically warning that the superintendent exception process would create racial inequities because immigrant families face language and cultural barriers to requesting special exceptions.

publicComments.name: Ting Cheng Wang
publicComments.summary: Opposed the policy, noting that it effectively forces parents to choose their child's middle school at age five, which is an unfair burden given that only non-choice school families retain access to both Kennedy and North Star.

publicComments.name: Megan O'Reilly Green
publicComments.summary: Expressed nuanced support for pausing the policy and argued that the fundamental problem is North Star's structure and the perception that it is the 'best' school, stating she does not believe the proposed policy would harm Mandarin Immersion recruitment and that the district's real challenge is North Star's incompatibility with its equity mission.

publicComments.name: Elizabeth Flavel
publicComments.summary: As an active Adelante community member, shared her personal experience that the flexibility to try kindergarten with the option to transfer gave her family the confidence to enroll in Spanish Immersion, and cautioned the board about potential unintended consequences of the policy on incoming family decisions.

publicComments.name: Maureen McPeek
publicComments.summary: A second-grade teacher of 25 years argued that the current safety plan is inadequate, that behavior management would dominate in-person instruction, and asked the board to wait until January 2021 to reopen.

publicComments.name: Jackie Moore
publicComments.summary: A special day class teacher described positive outcomes in distance learning for her students, warned that reopening could cause preventable COVID deaths, and urged the board to continue distance learning until a vaccine is available.

publicComments.name: Nusheen Kavyani
publicComments.summary: A community member argued that the reopening plans presented show all the risks of COVID with no benefit of in-person learning, and requested the public chat be kept open to simulate a true public meeting.

publicComments.name: Mercedes Kwiatkowski
publicComments.summary: A child psychiatrist described alarming mental health trends she is observing clinically—depression, anxiety, suicidal ideation—and proposed launching a voluntary pilot in-person cohort using the district's four pillars framework.

publicComments.name: Sarah Henderson Martinez
publicComments.summary: A preschool teacher expressed concern about inadequate contact tracing in the district, the risk of bringing COVID home to elderly parents, and cautioned against attributing reduced learning outcomes solely to distance learning rather than the broader pandemic trauma.

publicComments.name: Kathryn Stewart
publicComments.summary: A parent argued that teachers should not have to choose between risking death from COVID and losing their income, and asked parents to be patient and support teachers by waiting for a vaccine before returning in person.

publicComments.name: Eric Sutton
publicComments.summary: A parent from Orion reported that 65% of first-grade families preferred distance learning and 100% wanted to keep their current teachers, praised the school's two-teacher distance model, and argued that mental illness should be attributed to COVID and poor air quality rather than distance learning.

publicComments.name: Christy Herrera
publicComments.summary: A kindergarten teacher of 20 years said true kindergarten teaching is impossible with COVID distancing protocols, argued five-year-olds cannot developmentally comply with masking and distancing requirements, and noted the stress of the past week had sent at least one teacher to the ER.

publicComments.name: Indra Hernandez
publicComments.summary: A preschool teacher expressed gratitude for the data collection, noted her students are engaging well in distance learning, and asked that preschool safety protocols be equivalent to those at the elementary level rather than treated separately.

publicComments.name: Ramin Kaivan
publicComments.summary: The husband of a preschool teacher and longtime Redwood City business owner said he has watched students flourish in distance learning in his own home and argued that sending young children who cannot comply with PPE back to school would be dangerous and irresponsible.

publicComments.name: Laura Atherton
publicComments.summary: A special day class preschool teacher explained that her students wear diapers, require hand-over-hand feeding and support, cannot wear masks due to sensory needs, and warned the board that physical distancing is impossible with her population, describing a recent COVID exposure involving a child under five.

publicComments.name: Mohammad Tabrizi
publicComments.summary: A parent argued it would be malpractice not to compare the district's reopening plan with neighboring districts like Menlo Park, which involved teachers and parents extensively, and said the district's lack of preparedness shakes trust in any eventual reopening.

publicComments.name: Christina Kim
publicComments.summary: A parent of a Mandarin immersion student at Orion reported that an independent parent survey showed high flexibility about reopening but strong desire to keep the Mandarin program intact, and asked the board to allow site-specific collaborative solutions.

publicComments.name: Christina Hsu
publicComments.summary: A Mandarin immersion parent said the district survey caused confusion and anxiety in her community, and asked the board to allow each site and program to work individually with administrators and teachers to create models that fit their specific community.

publicComments.name: Devin/Michelle Mullaney
publicComments.summary: The speaker yielded their time without making substantive remarks.

publicComments.name: Cameron Hoffman
publicComments.summary: A parent called for respectful, fact-based dialogue, noting the district must speak up for the most vulnerable students not attending remotely, shared that learning pods are working safely in their experience, and urged everyone to approach reopening as a dial rather than a switch.

publicComments.name: Linda Elkins
publicComments.summary: A parent of three district students offered feedback that the survey conflated multiple variables making it hard to parse preferences, expressed support for a return to in-person learning in either AM/PM or hybrid format, and suggested leveraging parent expertise more meaningfully.

publicComments.name: Melanie Haskell
publicComments.summary: A first-grade parent asked the board to preserve independent study as an ongoing option regardless of the in-person decision, and recommended the district engage parents as active contributors rather than just survey respondents, citing Menlo Park's model.

publicComments.name: Kerry Schmidt
publicComments.summary: A bilingual kindergarten teacher of 25 years said she longs to be in the classroom but argued that without proper safety measures, returning does not provide a safe learning environment, and that constant safety monitoring would severely detract from instruction.

publicComments.name: Kamli Nannini
publicComments.summary: A kindergarten parent who is also a physician with an epidemiology background argued that teachers are essential workers, that actual transmission risk from masked asymptomatic children is negligible, and urged the board to bring in medical experts to brief teachers before decisions are made.

publicComments.name: Megan O'Reilly Green
publicComments.summary: An Orion parent participation program parent expressed that public comments about their program being at risk caused instability and hurt recruitment, asked the board to stop characterizing Orion families as well-resourced, and called for a genuine partnership and plan for program stability.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
publicComments.summary: An Adelante Selby parent expressed disappointment at any threat to choice program continuity, supported teachers, and raised concerns about parents lying to contact tracers and about the district's buildings lacking adequate air filtration even for smoke, let alone COVID.

publicComments.name: Michelle Butler
publicComments.summary: A bilingual teacher who uses a wheelchair explained that in-person she would be in constant safety-policing mode rather than teaching, described starting the interactive process with apprehension, and emphasized that teachers working hard in distance learning truly love their students.

publicComments.name: Jeanette Serrano
publicComments.summary: A 37-year kindergarten teacher said classroom reconfiguration would restart the exhausting relationship-building process mid-year, that she cannot ensure her primarily Latinx families adhere to the four pillars outside school, and that she has photographic evidence of students in pods not wearing masks.

publicComments.name: Maria Stockton
publicComments.summary: A speaker suggested that if mental health concerns warrant in-person learning, older grades (2–5) who can better comply with masking and distancing should return first rather than the youngest children, and asked the board to wait at least through the end of the first trimester.

publicComments.name: Keely Vega
publicComments.summary: A kindergarten parent asked the board to respond to the suggestion of a voluntary pilot cohort for willing teachers and families, and asked how the district is leveraging the experiences of already-opened neighboring districts like Menlo Park.

2020-09-23-board-meeting.agendaChanges: The agenda was resequenced as follows: (1) Item 12.1 (Longevity Recognition) was moved up to before the public hearings (Section 9); (2) Action items 16.1, 16.2, and 16.3 were moved to the consent calendar; (3) Item 16.4 (Board Policy 6157) was kept as an action item for discussion. Sections 1-3 (Call to Order, Closed Session) occurred before the recording begins. The meeting transcript begins at the Reconvene/Roll Call (Section 4). Items were taken in order: Roll Call/Report Out (4), Welcome (5), Changes to Agenda (6), Approval of Agenda (7), Oral Communication (8), then 12.1 (moved up), then 9.1, 9.2, then 10 (bond consent), 11.1, 13.1, 14.1, then consent bundle (15.1, 15.2, 15.3, 16.1, 16.2, 16.3), then 16.4, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22.

items.title: Public Hearing on Learning Continuity and Attendance for 2020-21

publicComments.name: Ben Packer
publicComments.summary: A Roy Cloud parent expressing strong support for the Learning Continuity Plan, particularly praising that schools and students with higher needs were being prioritized.

Discussion on COVID-19

publicComments.name: Whitney Glockner Black
publicComments.summary: A Roosevelt parent raised concerns about students accessing inappropriate and addictive content on school-issued devices via YouTube, and urged the district to implement stronger content restrictions promptly.

publicComments.name: Katherine Stewart
publicComments.summary: A parent stated she does not want her child to return to school until a vaccine is available, preferring the inconvenience of distance learning over the risk of death or serious illness.

publicComments.name: Michelle Gagliardi
publicComments.summary: A parent of a kindergartner in an SDC class argued that children with disabilities often cannot wear masks or social distance, making in-person return unsafe and not worth the risk.

publicComments.name: Mercedes Kwiatkowski
publicComments.summary: A community psychiatrist expressed support for a cautious, phased return to school, noting children are struggling mentally and emotionally, while emphasizing teacher involvement and safety in any reopening plan.

publicComments.name: Christy Herrera
publicComments.summary: A district teacher expressed feeling blindsided by the reopening discussion, warned about health risks of returning during flu season and with community spread, and urged the board to reconsider any return to campus.

publicComments.name: Sandy Hoover
publicComments.summary: A TK teacher in a high-risk health category described the physical realities of working with young children and bodily fluids, expressed fear of contracting COVID and transmitting it to her newborn grandson, and criticized the inadequacy of PPE provided.

publicComments.name: Keri Yen
publicComments.summary: A parent of three, including a kindergartner with an IEP for speech, expressed strong support for returning younger students to in-person learning as soon as possible, noting that distance learning is driving inequity and some families have already left the district.

publicComments.name: Cameron Hoffman
publicComments.summary: A parent of two district students supported in-person learning for the neediest children, urged the district to learn from neighboring schools already reopening, and emphasized that the risks of staying home outweigh the risks of returning for the most vulnerable students.

publicComments.name: Allison Oldford
publicComments.summary: A parent of a fourth grader and two first graders stated that supporting teacher safety and returning students to in-person learning are not mutually exclusive, and shared that her children have been safely attending childcare in-person since June.

publicComments.name: Brian Crawford
publicComments.summary: A father of three described his son's emotional deterioration under distance learning, including a disturbing comment about wishing to be dead, and urged the board not to wait to return students to campus.

publicComments.name: Janelle Allen
publicComments.summary: An immunocompromised parent of a Roy Cloud kindergartner strongly opposed returning to school in October, urging the board to wait until after flu season and warning that young children cannot reliably wear masks or social distance.

publicComments.name: Jennifer Wenzel
publicComments.summary: A district teacher of 13 years expressed deep concern about returning to school given Redwood City's high COVID infection rate, citing data on children's higher viral loads and the risk of transmission to families and staff.

publicComments.name: Jeanette Serrano
publicComments.summary: A 34-year kindergarten teacher expressed alarm at the proposal to return TK–1 students, noting that young children cannot reliably wear masks, that teachers in these grades face high exposure risk, and that Redwood City schools have particularly vulnerable populations.

publicComments.name: Sarah Latora
publicComments.summary: A parent expressed preference for the AM/PM cohort option over the two-day-a-week model, raised concerns about funding for cleaning between sessions, and requested a clear trigger plan for switching back to distance learning if cases surge.

publicComments.name: Susan Forker
publicComments.summary: An Adelante Selby parent strongly opposed removing students from dual language immersion programs as part of any reopening plan, and urged the district to survey immersion families separately with full information about potential program disruption.

publicComments.name: Eric Sutton
publicComments.summary: A first-grade parent expressed that while he was not opposing reopening outright, disrupting students' current distance learning classrooms and teacher relationships mid-semester would be harmful and deeply unfair to children who are thriving in their current setting.

Discussion on COVID-19

publicComments.name: Michelle Torito
publicComments.summary: Described teachers working 10-12 hour days including weekends due to excessive planning, grading, emails, meetings, and state mandates, calling the situation unsustainable and asking for schedule changes and workload relief.

publicComments.name: Christina Granado
publicComments.summary: A third-grade teacher described an overwhelming workload even when splitting lessons with teammates, questioned the need for the additional attendance form, and asked the board for support to help teachers do their jobs well.

publicComments.name: Vanessa Sanchez
publicComments.summary: A second-grade teacher reported averaging 82.5 hours of work per week—50 hours above her contracted 32.5—and said the administration's suggestion that most tasks could be done in the 6.5-hour Wednesday prep day was a blatant disregard of teachers' reality.

publicComments.name: Rita Melton
publicComments.summary: Described lesson preparation now taking four to five times longer than in person, said the district's letter response to RCTA's concerns was devoid of compassion, and asked why teachers are not treated with the same empathy they show students.

publicComments.name: Michelle Bond
publicComments.summary: A Northstar teacher said she was voluntarily working 12-hour days plus weekends, described physical symptoms of stress, and argued that every minute spent on clerical tasks like the new attendance requirements takes away from quality instruction for students.

publicComments.name: Sharon Tsuchiyama
publicComments.summary: Described spending four hours planning each lesson and working 10-plus hours a day seven days a week, and asked for scheduling flexibility to hold more small groups, use office hours differently, and build stronger student relationships.

publicComments.name: Megan Blackburn
publicComments.summary: A fifth-grade teacher at Roosevelt said the current rigid schedule is not good for 10-year-olds, described being unable to complete planning, grading, attendance, and parent communication within contracted hours, and called the workload unsustainable.

publicComments.name: Monica Voltz
publicComments.summary: Said teachers are working a minimum of 10-hour days seven days a week, described students being pushed through link after link rather than feeling nurtured, and urged the board to provide support and a lighter workload.

publicComments.name: Heather Irani
publicComments.summary: A 31-year veteran first-grade bilingual teacher at Hoover described spending Labor Day and another full day working at school with her college-age daughter's help, and said the workload is simply not sustainable.

publicComments.name: Chelsea Williams
publicComments.summary: A middle school math teacher described being live with students or in office hours from 8:15 to 2:00 with 40-plus emails and 50-plus comments to address daily, said lessons take four to five times longer to create, and stated that working 30-plus hours beyond contracted hours each week is not sustainable.

publicComments.name: Sandy Hoover
publicComments.summary: A TK teacher at Clifford described putting in 12-hour days five days a week just to have weekends with family, listed numerous duties impossible to complete in a seven-hour day, and said the situation is causing anxiety, poor sleep, and is not sustainable long term.

publicComments.name: Christy Herrera
publicComments.summary: A kindergarten teacher in her 20th year described working a minimum of 12 hours a day every day including weekends, said tech troubleshooting alone adds hours daily, and urged the board to explore using existing platforms like Google Classroom and Seesaw to eliminate redundant reporting forms.

publicComments.name: Stephanie Pappalardo
publicComments.summary: A middle school RSP teacher advocated for her special education students, describing them as overwhelmed by multiple digital platforms and excessive screen time, and asked the board to reconsider requiring students to do additional work on Wednesdays.

publicComments.name: Erin Washburn
publicComments.summary: As RCTA president, presented survey data showing 75% of members working more than nine hours above contracted hours and 43% working more than 16 hours above, called the workload a grievable contract violation, and said schedules and attendance tracking must be simplified.

publicComments.name: Angie Teeple
publicComments.summary: A fifth-grade teacher with 28 years of experience described teachers working six to seven days a week at 12 hours a day, gave the example of spending 90 minutes just to format a single math problem online, and asked the board to acknowledge that teacher well-being is valued.

publicComments.name: Katherine Rivera
publicComments.summary: As principal of Orion Alternative, offered a silver lining by praising the teachers' amazing work and highlighting how two previously divided parent groups have united this year in supporting both students and staff.

publicComments.name: Mohammed Tabrizi
publicComments.summary: A parent expressed disappointment that the district did not switch from Google Meet back to Zoom over the summer despite requests made last spring, calling the delay inexcusable and saying it worsened an already difficult learning environment.

publicComments.name: Tia Knoedler
publicComments.summary: A special education instructional assistant in her eighth year at Roosevelt said the workload is oppressive, demoralizing, and borderline persecutory after only three weeks, and urged the board to prioritize teachers' needs over any parent-driven agenda.

publicComments.name: Marlena Goanna Mendoza
publicComments.summary: A special education teacher described going to a student's home on her prep day to teach him how to navigate his iPad and read a clock, training her aides in technology, and said countless small but necessary tasks leave her with no time despite working long hours.

publicComments.name: Shannon Cody
publicComments.summary: Expressed love for the district and its people, acknowledged the difficulty of distance learning, and called for the whole community—teachers, administrators, board, and families—to work together toward what is best for students because none can do it alone.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
