Changes to the Agenda
Approval of Agenda (Action Required)
Oral Communication

publicComments.name: John Langbein
publicComments.summary: Requested the district allow bicycle and pedestrian access through Selby Lane School grounds, which had been blocked by chain-link fencing since the shelter-in-place order, arguing the route is a vital and safe passage for the neighborhood.

items.title: Second Public Hearing: Neighborhood Schools Attendance Boundary Change Study

publicComments.name: Matt Collier
publicComments.summary: Requested that the board restore the Roy Cloud boundary to include both sides of Alameda de las Pulgas on a two-block section near Jefferson, as Scenario 3 moved his home to the Clifford zone, splitting siblings.

publicComments.name: William Paxton
publicComments.summary: Expressed support for Scenario 3 and thanked the board and consultants, noting that the Kentwood/Atherwood neighborhood lacks a pedestrian-friendly path to Garfield and is better served by Henry Ford.

publicComments.name: Anna Fonseca Janoff
publicComments.summary: Asked whether the goal of redistricting is to move away from neighborhood schools toward larger comprehensive campuses and whether any scenario would bring back families currently attending charter or private schools, also questioning how Roy Cloud's low socioeconomic diversity would be addressed through school of choice.

publicComments.name: Michael Bergeron
publicComments.summary: Advocated for assigning Woodside Hills to Roy Cloud, arguing it would encourage private school families to return to public school and that community feedback supporting this change was not reflected in Scenario 3.

publicComments.name: Brian Varese
publicComments.summary: Expressed preference for Scenario 2, questioned whether the school of choice lottery promotes diversity intentionally, and asked the board to address schools approaching 80% capacity in Scenarios 1 and 3.

publicComments.name: Selena Holder
publicComments.summary: Supported Scenario 2, arguing that leaving Roy Cloud unchanged wastes its capacity and reputation, and that rezoning the Selby Lane neighborhood to Roy Cloud would bring private school families back to public school and improve diversity.

publicComments.name: Keely Vega
publicComments.summary: Strongly supported Scenario 2, arguing that relying on a school of choice lottery to achieve diversity is superficial, that walkability should not be a guiding criterion, and that the board should explain how it plans to weigh the community survey results showing 88% support for Scenario 2.

publicComments.name: Patricia Boers
publicComments.summary: Supported Scenario 2 or a tweak to Scenario 3 to include Woodside Hills in Roy Cloud, noting her family's isolation in the neighborhood, a desire for a K-8 community school, and that Woodside Hills has ethnic diversity that would benefit Roy Cloud.

publicComments.name: Colleen Voss
publicComments.summary: Co-signed the Selby Lane community letter supporting Scenario 2 and questioned why Scenario 3, described as the community input scenario, largely followed Scenario 1 and left Roy Cloud unchanged despite 88% survey support for Scenario 2.

publicComments.name: Frank Hannig
publicComments.summary: Supported Scenario 2 or a minor tweak to Scenario 3 to include Woodside Hills in Roy Cloud, arguing that geographic barriers make Henry Ford less accessible and that the change would encourage families to move from private to public school.

publicComments.name: David Eichler
publicComments.summary: Supported Scenario 2, arguing it provides the most balance for the district and that a K-8 school like Roy Cloud would foster a stronger sense of community in Woodside Hills, encouraging families to leave private schools.

publicComments.name: Lindsay Temple
publicComments.summary: Supported Scenario 2 or a tweak to Scenario 3 assigning Woodside Hills to Roy Cloud, stating it would help build a sub-community and that the strong survey support for Scenario 2 should be honored.

publicComments.name: Abraham Fraley
publicComments.summary: Supported Scenario 2, citing safety concerns about crossing a state highway to reach Henry Ford, the need for demographic continuity with the Roy Cloud census area, and expressing frustration that school of choice is being prioritized over rezoned neighborhoods.

publicComments.name: Lisa Carr
publicComments.summary: Supported Scenario 2 or a Scenario 3 tweak including Woodside Hills in Roy Cloud, noting the neighborhood's approximately 30 children do not attend public school and that the community financially supports Redwood City schools.

publicComments.name: Katha Hall
publicComments.summary: Supported Scenario 2 as it would create community for Woodside Hills children, noted the neighborhood has ethnic diversity, and argued the board should respect the 88% survey support for Scenario 2.

items.title: Consent Items (Action Required) — as amended (10.1 pulled)
Approval of Measure U Application for 2020-21, Connect Community Charter School (PULLED)
Action Items — 11.1: Adoption of Roadmap to Reopening / My School Plan

publicComments.name: Kathryn Stewart
publicComments.summary: Asked for specific details on how many hours per day Mandarin immersion instruction would occur live in Mandarin, and cautioned against informal play dates as a substitute for school given ongoing transmission risks.

publicComments.name: Brian Cagle
publicComments.summary: Advocated for continuing PE instruction during distance learning, noting students' need for physical activity, and cautioned that outdoor learning would face significant challenges from heat, cold, and wet conditions.

publicComments.name: Christy Herrera
publicComments.summary: Called for testing and contact tracing before campus return, asked that teachers have the option to teach from home or classroom, and recommended waiting at least 14 days after holiday gatherings before any return to campus.

publicComments.name: Michelle Torito
publicComments.summary: Supported the distance learning decision as the safest choice and asked the board to consider postponing any school reopening until at least January 19, at least 14 days after winter break, to avoid holiday gathering-related spikes.

publicComments.name: Sandy Hoover
publicComments.summary: Emphasized that testing, tracking, and tracing protocols must be in place before any return to campus, and expressed hope to meet her TK students in person at some point during the year.

publicComments.name: Jamie Guiana
publicComments.summary: Echoed support for including PE in the distance learning curriculum, arguing that physical activity is essential for students' cardiovascular, social, and mental health during a sedentary distance learning period.

publicComments.name: Julia Feng
publicComments.summary: Thanked the board and staff for considering special needs students in planning, and asked what tools and strategies would be available to help parents support the youngest learners who may struggle to engage with distance learning on screens.

publicComments.name: Kayla Kamley
publicComments.summary: As a first grade teacher, urged the district to distribute technology and hotspots to families early, provide physical books for younger grades, supply basic school materials, and communicate class assignments and daily schedules to families ahead of the school start.

publicComments.name: Sarah La Torre
publicComments.summary: Proposed that the district adopt a model similar to the public library's art program — providing take-home supply kits — to support GATE enrichment during distance learning and maintain a sense of normalcy for students.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
publicComments.summary: Asked when child care options would be made available to families, requested that highest-need students be prioritized in child care placements, advocated for providing home supply kits and early technology distribution to avoid loss of instructional time.

publicComments.name: Teresita Carlos
publicComments.summary: Thanked the board and staff, shared her personal decision to keep her child home through the winter break to limit exposure, and praised Henry Ford teachers for their excellent and multifaceted distance learning during the spring.

items.title: Action Items — 11.2: Recommendation for Review and Approval of the 2020 Uniform Complaint Procedures Meeting Federal Requirements
Information — 12.1: Quarterly Williams Report, 4th Quarter, April 1, 2020 through June 30, 2020
Adjournment

B.name: Liz Wolf
B.role: Assistant Superintendent of Educational Services (presenter); also used for unidentified/background voices

C.name: María Díaz-Slocum
C.role: Trustee (also Dr. Baker, Superintendent, shares label in some exchanges)

D.name: Eliana (staff)

Items 10.3 (Facilities Use Agreement with Connect Community Charter School) and 10.4 (Joint Use Agreement with Little Budz/BuildingKidz) were moved from Action Items to the Consent Calendar. Section 7 (Bond Program Action Items) and Section 9 (Consent Items) had no items and were skipped. The meeting proceeded in agenda order otherwise.

items.title: Call to Order / Roll Call
Welcome by the School Board President
Changes to the Agenda
Approval of Agenda
Oral Communication
Bond Program Consent Items
Discussion of Roadmap to Reopening My School Plan

publicComments.name: Pam Barish
publicComments.summary: A 30-year kindergarten teacher urged the board to be attentive to the pandemic's trajectory and be willing to put reopening plans on hold until it is safe.

publicComments.name: Laura Easley
publicComments.summary: An education professional and parent advocated for increased focus on socio-emotional learning, support for marginalized students, and collaboration with after-school providers.

publicComments.name: Dr. Mercedes Kwiakowski
publicComments.summary: A board-certified pediatrician and child psychiatrist recommended balanced health metrics beyond COVID cases, prioritization of K-2 for in-person learning, full-day classroom instruction if returning, and a community commitment to minimizing outside exposures.

publicComments.name: Gloria Comfort
publicComments.summary: A parent raised unanswered questions about what happens when a teacher or student tests positive, including quarantine, substitutes, testing costs, and family notification.

publicComments.name: Jeanette Melendez
publicComments.summary: A fifth-grade teacher at Adelante Selby said she loves teaching but is not comfortable returning in person given rising COVID cases and asked the board to choose full distance learning.

publicComments.name: Susan Forker
publicComments.summary: A community member thanked the board, asked when the decision for August would be made even under a hybrid plan, and urged greater focus on equity and childcare solutions including use of school sites.

publicComments.name: Erin Washburn
publicComments.summary: The newly elected RCTA president and middle school music teacher said too many questions remain unanswered for in-person models and asked the board not to force teachers out of five months of sheltering in place when COVID numbers are higher than ever.

publicComments.name: Ariana Baltay
publicComments.summary: An 8th-grade teacher with over 20 years in the district said schools should not reopen given unsafe conditions and identified four major unresolved problems including inadequate testing, restroom safety, lunch protocols, and incorrect application of stable cohort guidelines.

publicComments.name: Jennifer Overby
publicComments.summary: A teacher and parent asked the board to protect all essential school workers, including custodians, office staff, and nurses, and suggested pre-return COVID testing similar to required TB tests.

publicComments.name: Caitlin McManus
publicComments.summary: A middle school teacher and parent said she cannot wait to return to face-to-face teaching but only if safe, and urged consideration of full distance learning given risks and the decisions of neighboring districts.

publicComments.name: Michelle Packet
publicComments.summary: A teacher cited concerns including lack of hot running water, asymptomatic spread, inadequate PPE, inability to monitor social distancing, special education challenges, and the possibility that COVID is airborne with inadequate HVAC systems.

publicComments.name: Melanie Haskell
publicComments.summary: A parent thanked the board for including social pods, asked for more specific information about both learning models before families must decide, raised questions about positive case protocols and schedule flexibility for distance learning, suggested independent study as a third option, and advocated for maintaining librarian services.

publicComments.name: Peggy Klein
publicComments.summary: An RSP teacher at Kennedy Middle School noted that Step 1 requirements for flat/declining COVID cases are not met, warned that many teachers may not return and substitute shortages will worsen, and argued distance learning would provide higher quality, more consistent instruction.

publicComments.name: Candice Nance Kerrigan
publicComments.summary: A parent of an incoming kindergartner asked the board to address what teacher training has occurred over the summer and whether parents could serve as digital volunteers.

publicComments.name: Sandy Hoover
publicComments.summary: A TK teacher at Clifford raised unanswered questions about when schools would close due to COVID cases, tracking of student illnesses, and proposed starting with distance learning for one to two months to assess case trends before returning.

publicComments.name: Ellie Kolekar
publicComments.summary: A parent sought clarity on what 100% distance learning looks like for families who opt out of in-person, whether distance learners can participate in school virtual events, and whether school-of-choice status would be preserved if a family chooses distance learning.

publicComments.name: Jennifer Mercado
publicComments.summary: A teacher said returning to in-person instruction is not safe given health recommendations and rising case counts, and urged 100% distance learning until the rate drops below 100 cases per 100,000.

publicComments.name: Erin Washburn
publicComments.summary: Speaking with time ceded by seven colleagues, Erin expanded on her earlier comments arguing that the roadmap plan cannot hold up in practice, that full distance learning gives students more daily access to teachers and peers than the hybrid model, and that teachers have trained all summer to deliver high-quality distance learning.

publicComments.name: Jocelyn Rutenberg Stemke
publicComments.summary: A parent of an incoming fifth grader said she is not comfortable with in-person learning given rising cases and unknowns, and urged starting the year with distance-only learning, noting children would be miserable under restrictive in-person conditions anyway.

publicComments.name: Ann Lucero
publicComments.summary: A teacher argued that sending students back in the hybrid model wastes an opportunity to embrace transformative distance learning and characterized the hybrid approach as a dangerous half-measure.

publicComments.name: Christina Kim
publicComments.summary: A parent of an incoming Mandarin Immersion kindergartner said her family needs full distance learning due to compromised family members and asked for detailed information about what distance learning, especially for immersion programs, would look like.

publicComments.name: Unidentified speaker
publicComments.summary: A first-grade teacher at Clifford recommended the distance learning model, citing risks of transmission, logistical and social-emotional difficulties of in-person protocols for young children, and ongoing rising case counts in Redwood City.

publicComments.name: Ellen Jacobson
publicComments.summary: A parent and teacher thanked the board for following the data and urged redirecting PPE and protocol funding toward technology access and robust online learning.

publicComments.name: Tiffany Anderson
publicComments.summary: A Henry Ford parent and former teacher said she is hesitant to send children back or put teachers at risk given firsthand experience with COVID devastation and unclear science.

publicComments.name: Betsy Snow
publicComments.summary: The library media specialist at Sequoia High School supported distance learning and strongly opposed eliminating any librarian positions, emphasizing librarians' critical role in student literacy and their ability to provide services virtually.

publicComments.name: Megan O'Reilly Green
publicComments.summary: A small business owner, licensed home daycare provider, and Orion parent proposed that PTOs assist communities in developing equitable childcare solutions within educational cohorts during distance learning.

publicComments.name: Kathryn Stewart
publicComments.summary: A parent shared how she runs informal Zoom tutoring sessions for neighborhood children and encouraged other parents to volunteer time to support students and give working parents a break.

publicComments.name: Indra Hernandez
publicComments.summary: A preschool CDC teacher thanked the board, noted her program has been working hard on distance learning, reported one child in the hospital with COVID and a parent with symptoms, and asked for continuation of distance learning until it is safe.

publicComments.name: Rita Melissa
publicComments.summary: A teacher read a collective letter from 26 Adelante Selby teachers urging the board not to open in-person, expressing confidence in delivering robust full distance learning, and asking that the lives of teachers, students, and staff be prioritized.

publicComments.name: Maribel Parenti
publicComments.summary: An Adelante Selby staff member clarified that teachers do not refuse to work but are seeking safety for the entire community including bus drivers, custodians, cafeteria staff, and students.

publicComments.name: Eric Sutton
publicComments.summary: A Roosevelt/Orion teacher used probability math to illustrate COVID risk in social circles, and recommended small Zoom groups of five, daily teacher meetings with students, and support for social pods with co-located students sharing teachers and schedules.

publicComments.name: Katie Goetz
publicComments.summary: An immersion parent shifted her focus to asking for clear guidelines and support for non-native-speaking parents to help their children during full distance learning in the immersion program.

publicComments.name: Joan Perez
publicComments.summary: A 16-year district teacher and parent said there is not enough noise about teacher and staff safety, agreed with the board's direction, and noted that the WHO says schools can return only when the virus is suppressed, which is not currently the case.

publicComments.name: Julia Fang
publicComments.summary: A parent of Mandarin Immersion students asked how immersion can work effectively online for young children who struggle to pay attention to screens, and requested creative solutions for interactive, engaging immersion distance learning.

publicComments.name: Michelle Butler
publicComments.summary: A special education teacher and parent noted some special ed students made measurable goal progress during spring distance learning and expressed confidence in doing better this time, while noting the impossibility of maintaining six-foot distancing with young children in person.

publicComments.name: Maria Stockton
publicComments.summary: The CSEA president and 41-year district employee said she initially felt assured by reopening safety plans but growing case numbers have eroded that confidence, asked that classified staff be supported in online learning, and requested a process for notifying staff of COVID exposures on site.

publicComments.name: Josefina
publicComments.summary: A Henry Ford fifth-grade teacher and parent of an incoming fifth-grader said though distance learning is hard for families, it is necessary for safety, and urged the board to start fall with distance learning and revisit in spring based on data.

publicComments.name: Brian Howell
publicComments.summary: A Redwood City teacher said he cannot protect children's physical safety in person and expressed confidence that distance learning can be a high-quality, engaging experience that inspires student motivation.

publicComments.name: Jeanette Serrano
publicComments.summary: A 33-year kindergarten teacher in her final year said teachers give everything willingly but should not be asked to sacrifice their safety, and expressed confidence in the board for following the science.

publicComments.name: Franklin Herrera
publicComments.summary: A Kennedy School teacher and soccer coach said he misses students but does not want to risk the community's health and asked whether the district will be part of the solution or part of the problem.

publicComments.name: Vanessa Sanchez
publicComments.summary: A second-grade teacher at Adelante urged the board to begin remotely, argued that planning time should not be split across multiple models, and said assuming families can maintain stable cohorts outside school is inequitable.

publicComments.name: Sarah Gurkes Suba
publicComments.summary: A parent nursing a baby asked for flexibility in the distance learning schedule for dual-working households, mentioned independent study as a possible third option, and asked about keeping after-school cohorts together.

publicComments.name: Subha Mati
publicComments.summary: A high-risk district technology coach said choosing to work in person would put her health and family at risk, and urged the board to decide on distance learning early so teachers can focus their training without fear.

publicComments.name: David Weekley
publicComments.summary: A parent of an incoming Mandarin Immersion kindergartner and a toddler called for regular testing as a pillar of safe reopening and for more serious incorporation of outdoor instruction.

publicComments.name: Michelle Laird
publicComments.summary: The district psychologist and preschool special education coordinator, speaking for over 30 special ed preschool staff, said their severely disabled non-verbal students require hands-on teaching and are at high risk, and identified one of their students as the child who tested positive with COVID complications.

publicComments.name: Joan Solari
publicComments.summary: A teacher with 25 years of experience said as much as teachers want to be with students, the science does not support returning and they must follow the data.

publicComments.name: Jennifer Wenzel
publicComments.summary: A 13-year RCSD teacher and mother of a toddler said she has spent sleepless nights worrying about bringing COVID home to her family, and stated that opening schools under current community spread levels will likely cause deaths that could be prevented with 100% distance learning.

publicComments.name: Alex Herrera
publicComments.summary: A substitute teacher and son of a 20-year district veteran said returning to school would jeopardize the health of thousands, noted that prisons are releasing inmates and restaurants are closed indoors for similar reasons, and said he would not return as a sub given the risks.

publicComments.name: Michelle Mehrani
publicComments.summary: A 20-year kindergarten teacher at Clifford said nothing makes her sadder than not being with her new class, but it is currently unsafe for students, staff, and herself, and appreciated the board's comments.

publicComments.name: Sophie Mintyr
publicComments.summary: A parent acknowledged the enormous burdens on teachers under either model and urged the board to prioritize equity and additional childcare solutions for families who cannot work from home or afford outside help.

publicComments.name: Stephanie Kolsing
publicComments.summary: An Orion teacher and Superintendent's Advisory Council member said teachers are afraid to speak out, confirmed teachers want to be with students but it is not safe, and asked that teachers have access to classrooms during distance learning for recording, small groups, and materials.

publicComments.name: Alec Duncan
publicComments.summary: A community member said there is no vaccine, cure, or treatment for COVID-19 and that opening schools in August is not a good health choice for students, teachers, staff, or parents.

publicComments.name: Aida Ortiz De Aguilar
publicComments.summary: A preschool teacher speaking on behalf of colleagues asked the board to reconsider reopening the CDC and to allow preschool teachers to begin when the rest of the district begins, meanwhile continuing distance learning for which they are prepared.

publicComments.name: Zoe Wilson
publicComments.summary: An eighth-grade teacher said it is heartbreaking not to be with students but that distance learning is the best decision overall given current circumstances.

publicComments.name: Kayla Kamley
publicComments.summary: A first-year teacher moving to first grade said starting with masks and without seeing facial expressions would be more socially-emotionally harmful to young children than online learning where they can see smiling faces and build relationships with their teacher.

publicComments.name: Michelle Laird (Suzanne Dunlap)
publicComments.summary: Suzanne Dunlap supported full distance learning and expressed deep concern for the preschool special education program and the severely disabled three-year-olds whose teacher described a student currently hospitalized with COVID.

publicComments.name: Jacqueline Rodriguez
publicComments.summary: A community member read a statement from a parent group that met with district and county officials, advocating for a plan to address learning loss, individualized student support, special population services, guaranteed instructional minutes, and childcare and technology access, urging all parties to collaborate for high-quality education.

publicComments.name: Patricia Perla
publicComments.summary: A preschool teacher recommended continuing distance learning until the COVID curve declines and essential testing is available, noting how difficult it is to keep preschoolers distanced and masked, and sharing fears expressed by families about returning to the classroom.

publicComments.name: Maria Elena
publicComments.summary: A special education teacher said the data points toward distance learning as the safest option, affirmed that the hybrid planning work will not be wasted, and described witnessing a COVID-positive resident with children in the district being taken away, underscoring the need to test everyone before gathering at schools.

publicComments.name: Mercedes Northern
publicComments.summary: A parent asked whether teachers could provide live scheduled daily classes with student accountability, whether outdoor classes could be considered when schools reopen, and whether counseling resources for at-risk students could be offered.

publicComments.name: Michelle Bond
publicComments.summary: A teacher briefly thanked the board and noted that just as districts prepared to pivot from hybrid to full distance learning, the reverse is equally true — starting with distance learning allows for a quick pivot back to in-person when safe.

publicComments.name: Elvira Cortez
publicComments.summary: A 21-year RCSD employee and Roosevelt CDC teacher and site supervisor questioned why child development centers would be kept open if regular schools are deemed unsafe, and urged reopening all programs together when it is safe for everyone.

publicComments.name: Gwen Minor
publicComments.summary: A community member cited CDC Director Redfield's warning that fall and winter 2020-21 will be among the most difficult periods in American public health, supported distance-only learning, and urged the community to collaborate on childcare solutions and developing the best online learning experience possible.

publicComments.name: Adriana Behar
publicComments.summary: A preschool teacher and Garfield parent said reopening preschool next Tuesday feels too soon given danger for young children, families, and teachers, and expressed confidence in the distance learning tools and training her team has acquired.

publicComments.name: James Carrick
publicComments.summary: A teacher and district parent said he did not become a teacher to save the economy, cited the death of a California teacher from COVID during summer school, and asked how many teachers or students must die before the board recognizes now is not the time to reopen.

publicComments.name: Abed Kamal
publicComments.summary: A Stanford instructor said it would be reckless to send children to school in the hybrid model given lack of testing, encouraged greater synchronous online teacher-student interaction based on his Zoom teaching experience, and suggested regular pool testing for when in-person teaching resumes.

publicComments.name: Michelle Ortez
publicComments.summary: A 21-year Roy Cloud teacher and RCSD parent said this should be parents and teachers versus COVID, not parents versus teachers, and asked community members to redirect negative energy toward contacting county and state officials for more childcare help.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
publicComments.summary: A parent proposed leveraging childcare partnerships and subsidies so schools could serve as safe childcare hubs for the most vulnerable families during full distance learning, and shared positive experience with a UC Berkeley hybrid synchronous/asynchronous summer program.

publicComments.name: Ariandi Jimenez
publicComments.summary: A new Hoover third-grade teacher expressed deep concern about disproportionate COVID impact on Latinx communities, noting 49% of San Mateo County cases are Latinx while Latinx residents are under 25% of the county population, and said returning to school in person would place her predominantly Latinx school community at greater risk.

publicComments.name: Linda Elkins
publicComments.summary: A parent of three elementary students said she is in the minority advocating for the hybrid model, cited safe summer camp experiences following county guidelines, and asked the board to approve both models now so the hybrid option is ready to activate quickly when conditions allow.

publicComments.name: Allie Gilmore
publicComments.summary: A parent of an incoming third-grader and a preschooler receiving special ed therapy said she is disappointed by the direction but hears the teachers, and urged the board to prioritize parent and family support, flesh out distance learning details, and help parents advocate for the district's needs.

publicComments.name: Rob Hager
publicComments.summary: A parent of two incoming fifth-graders supported full distance learning, raised questions about teacher testing frequency, thresholds for switching models, protocols for students running temperatures, and whether charter schools must follow district directives.

publicComments.name: Sylvia Antonelli
publicComments.summary: A third-grade teacher at Adelante Selby said the model of instruction is reversible but loss of human life is not, and expressed appreciation for board members and community members supporting 100% distance learning.

publicComments.name: Suzanne Ruiz Dunlap
publicComments.summary: A Railway State School District employee supported full distance learning and asked the board to continue advocating for the preschool special education teachers and CDC programs.

Item 14.6 (Approval of School Plan for Student Achievement) was recommended for consent but kept as an action item due to anticipated public comment cards (which ultimately did not materialize for that item specifically). Section 11 (Bond Program Action Items) had no items. Section 17 (Correspondence) was addressed briefly. The meeting proceeded largely in agenda order after the consent restructuring.

publicComments.name: Kathryn Stewart
publicComments.summary: Asked about the number of interactive teaching hours for Mandarin immersion students online, requested a diagram showing how 12 students would be physically distanced in a classroom, and questioned when parents would need to commit to distance-only learning so classroom numbers could be planned.

publicComments.name: Linda Elkins
publicComments.summary: Shared that Contra Costa County's June 22 guidance allowed 4 feet between students (rather than 6) and masks strongly encouraged but not required for ages 3–11, suggesting the board look into whether San Mateo County could adopt similar guidelines.

publicComments.name: Maria Stockton
publicComments.summary: Speaking as a CSEA representative, thanked Wendy Kelly for a productive meeting, urged the district not to lay off classified staff given temporary funding uncertainties, and described creative ideas to redeploy classified employees to support both online and on-site needs.

publicComments.name: Katie Goetz
publicComments.summary: Clarified that PTO funds raised for Mandarin immersion teacher coaches and classroom aides must run through the district as employee costs, and that the budget line items do not fully reflect total spending and donations benefiting both Orion and Mandarin immersion programs.

publicComments.name: Sarah Rutherford
publicComments.summary: As Orion Mandarin Immersion site council member, explained that PTO fundraising is intentionally structured to avoid Title 1 dependency for personnel, noted the high cost of language immersion programs, and described why classroom aides are critical given the non-participation norm for non-Mandarin-speaking parents.

publicComments.name: Megan O'Reilly Green
publicComments.summary: As interim co-president of Starship PTO, pushed back on social media accusations of inequitable funding between Orion and Mandarin immersion, explained that raised funds benefit the whole campus community, and asked the district to help address the optics issue.

publicComments.name: Amy Wang
publicComments.summary: As a Mandarin immersion parent, cautioned the community against drawing conclusions from a few budget line items without understanding the full picture of funding sources, and urged people to avoid divisive, inaccurate statements online.

publicComments.name: Sue Jorgensen
publicComments.summary: Identified herself as the author of the Facebook post in question, defended her comments as professional and factual, expressed genuine concern about funding equity across programs and schools including Taft and Northstar, and shared positive news about librarians partnering with the Redwood City Public Library for community reads.

items.title: Call to Order / Roll Call
Welcome by the School Board President
Changes to the Agenda
Approval of Agenda
Oral Communication
First of Two Public Hearings: School Attendance Boundary Change for Neighborhood Schools

publicComments.name: Kathryn Stewart
publicComments.summary: Suggested pooling all school funding into a lottery system where families choose preferred schools and the least-chosen schools are remodeled after the top choices.

publicComments.name: Roberta Land
publicComments.summary: Expressed strong opposition to reassigning her Kentfield Commons neighborhood to Garfield, arguing it is not a walkable neighborhood school and that Henry Ford is a more sensible option.

publicComments.name: Katherine Hagman
publicComments.summary: Voiced strong support for Scenario 2 and keeping the Mount Carmel neighborhood together at one school, noting Clifford is safer to bike to than Roosevelt for their area.

publicComments.name: Megan Maxwell Bay
publicComments.summary: Asked whether students currently attending a school of choice (Roy Cloud) would be grandfathered in and allowed to stay under any boundary change.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
publicComments.summary: Urged the board to include demographic data in its boundary analysis to avoid de facto segregation and to ensure more equitable distribution across schools.

publicComments.name: Meredith Park
publicComments.summary: Advocated for safe, walkable neighborhood schools, criticized the schools-of-choice model for fracturing community bonds, and urged the board to rethink the broader school model to build neighborhood cohesion.

publicComments.name: Cameron Matthews
publicComments.summary: Asked how population trend projections were derived, requested walkability mapping resources for boundary fringe areas, and inquired whether additional scenario options beyond Scenarios 1 and 2 would be presented.

publicComments.name: David Knox
publicComments.summary: Asked for clarification on whether current Roy Cloud district residents would be reassigned to another school under the proposed boundary changes.

publicComments.name: Eurydia Aquino
publicComments.summary: Asked whether her children, who attend a district school on an inter-district transfer while she works for the district, would be affected by boundary changes or need to reapply each year.

publicComments.name: Andreas and Ann Hildebrandt
publicComments.summary: Shared that moving their son from private school to Roy Cloud created a wonderful sense of community in Woodside Hills and voiced strong support for Scenario 2.

publicComments.name: Stephen Gay
publicComments.summary: Asked about the rationale for a small boundary cutout on the Goodwin/Fernside side of Alameda in Scenario 2, citing safety concerns about crossing a high-traffic area for such a small number of students.

publicComments.name: Denise Hannig
publicComments.summary: Argued that children in her neighborhood could safely walk to Roy Cloud without crossing major roads, making it a better fit than Henry Ford.

publicComments.name: Bill Brown
publicComments.summary: Asked whether PTO funding impacts had been analyzed in relation to the proposed boundary changes, expressing concern about unintended equity consequences, and thanked the superintendent and trustee for their capacity work.

publicComments.name: Matt Scott
publicComments.summary: Asked for clarification on which school his Nimitz Avenue address falls under in each scenario, learning he would be assigned to Garfield in Scenario 1 and Hoover in Scenario 2.

publicComments.name: George Lewis
publicComments.summary: Voiced support for Scenario 2 as a Woodside Hills resident, noting it would encourage more neighbors to move their children from private schools into Redwood City public schools and build community.

publicComments.name: Steve (Axed)
publicComments.summary: Voiced support for Scenario 2 as a Selby Lane neighborhood resident seeking continuity and community for his daughter after transitioning through Henry Ford.

items.title: Discussion on COVID-19

publicComments.name: Sarah Yarder
publicComments.summary: Questioned whether temperature checks are evidence-based given that many COVID-positive patients show no fever, and asked about UV-C lamp disinfection as used in hospitals.

publicComments.name: Lily O'Connor
publicComments.summary: Expressed concern about lack of clear, timely communication from the district and raised specific logistical questions about coordinating siblings on different school schedules under a hybrid model.

publicComments.name: Michelle Ortez
publicComments.summary: Asked whether the teacher survey results would be shared with the board and public, and if so, when.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
publicComments.summary: Recommended keeping child care cohorts aligned with classroom cohorts to avoid cross-mingling, and offered to connect the district with her health literacy research team to improve communication.

publicComments.name: Michelle and Devin Mulaney
publicComments.summary: Asked whether the district had explored contingency plans for greater openness, temporary union MoUs to allow volunteers, and a sliding scale giving younger students more in-person time to support language immersion.

publicComments.name: Becky Fouch
publicComments.summary: Asked whether any closed school campuses could be used for child care or expanded student capacity.

publicComments.name: Linda Elkins
publicComments.summary: Expressed surprise that a five-days-a-week option was not included in the survey, asked how quickly the district could pivot if county guidelines loosen, and noted a survey design issue that prevented selecting multiple child care options.

publicComments.name: Indra Hernandez
publicComments.summary: As a CDC preschool teacher, raised concerns about social distancing with young children, cleaning burdens on staff, the impracticality of a two-day in-person model for working families, and uncertainty about changing job descriptions.

publicComments.name: Dina Meyers
publicComments.summary: Asked what support was being provided to teachers developing distance-learning curriculum and questioned what meaningful social interaction could look like for students under strict social distancing requirements.

items.title: Discussion of School Plan for Student Achievement
Consent Items
Adoption of Resolution 32 Calling for an Election on November 3, 2020 for Election of 3 Members of the Governing Board
Approval of Permanently Renaming the 170 Selby Lane Site as Adelante Selby Spanish Immersion School

publicComments.name: Anuj Gagar
publicComments.summary: Urged the board to approve Adelante Selby, arguing it has a clear rationale, no negative connotations, and a clear community mandate from two votes.

publicComments.name: Araceli Tamayo
publicComments.summary: Urged approval of Adelante Selby, explaining that it honors both merged school communities and their combined traditions, and will help the community move forward and continue investing in the school.

publicComments.name: Maurice Willis
publicComments.summary: Strongly supported the Adelante Selby name, stating that the community's unified desire for the name demonstrates healing and forward movement that should be acknowledged by the board.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
publicComments.summary: Expressed frustration with the naming policy process and questioned whether, given the national reckoning with racial justice, the board was comfortable keeping a name referencing a wealthy white man on a Spanish immersion school, suggesting Unidos as a more representative alternative.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
publicComments.summary: Expressed frustration with the overall naming process, questioning why the community vote was not honored the first time and criticizing the process as undemocratic.

publicComments.name: Jonathan Cantu
publicComments.summary: Expressed disappointment that the board initially rejected the naming recommendation and voiced concern about the community losing both their campus and potentially their name.

publicComments.name: Chris Rosone
publicComments.summary: Affirmed community attachment to the Adelante Selby identity and expressed concern that the loudest voices at the end of the process had overtaken the broader community's wishes.

publicComments.name: Devin Mulaney
publicComments.summary: Urged the board to wrap up the naming process quickly given other pressing priorities, and noted that 'Mid Peninsula' could be confused with a nearby continuation school.

items.title: Call to Order / Roll Call
Welcome by the School Board President / Pledge of Allegiance
Changes to the Agenda
Approval of Agenda
Oral Communication
Discussion on the Safe Reopening of RCSD School Campuses this Fall of 2020-21

publicComments.name: Katherine Stewart
publicComments.summary: Asked about re-establishing transfer students, requested Mandarin immersion instruction at least three days a week, and urged the district to survey parents about work schedules to inform hybrid scheduling.

publicComments.name: Christy Herrera
publicComments.summary: A kindergarten teacher with 19 years of experience expressed concerns about the hybrid model for kindergartners and advocated for 100% distance learning for TK/K students at the start of the year.

publicComments.name: Sarah Latora
publicComments.summary: A parent of two praised Clifford Elementary staff, raised concerns about mask barriers for students with vision issues, requested consistent sibling scheduling, and supported team teaching and a district-wide virtual classroom for full-distance families.

publicComments.name: Sandy Hoover
publicComments.summary: A TK teacher at Clifford advocated for the full distance learning model to keep all staff, students, and families safe and to allow a positive introduction to school routines before returning in person.

publicComments.name: Jacqueline Rodriguez
publicComments.summary: A parent leader proposed the creation of a parent committee to collaborate with the district on decisions about returning to school.

publicComments.name: Alexandra Sawiski
publicComments.summary: An essential worker and single guardian expressed concerns about child care support for working parents and about support for non-Spanish-speaking families in the immersion program.

publicComments.name: Maria Elena Arteaga
publicComments.summary: A parent leader echoed the request for a parent committee to be involved in decisions about the return to school.

publicComments.name: Jenny Overby
publicComments.summary: A teacher thanked Dr. Baker and the board for the professional development week and asked what the plan is for regular COVID-19 testing of all district staff.

publicComments.name: Abid Kama
publicComments.summary: A Stanford instructor asked whether there is data comparing direct teacher instruction versus packaged online modules, and whether there is a plan to make up for lost instructional hours.

publicComments.name: Michelle S.M.
publicComments.summary: A speaker raised questions about why Fridays were chosen as the off day rather than Wednesday for cleaning, expressed concern about young children maintaining six-foot distancing, and stressed the need for child care solutions for both families and teachers.

publicComments.name: Mercedes Nording
publicComments.summary: A parent from Adelante and her husband Rex asked what the educational goals and expectations are for the new school year, whether grading will return, and whether there will be improved interactivity rather than just task assignment.

publicComments.name: Rosemary Campos
publicComments.summary: A parent asked why a five-day AM/PM program is not feasible, raised concerns about the immersion component in all three models, asked how decision-making committees were formed, and questioned how teachers with health concerns would be accommodated and how cleaning frequency would be addressed.

publicComments.name: Jen Cook
publicComments.summary: A parent of an incoming kindergartner expressed concern that none of the proposed models would be sustainable or effective for a child entering a Spanish immersion program via distance learning.

publicComments.name: Christopher Rosan
publicComments.summary: A parent from Adelante echoed concerns about kindergartners falling behind in language immersion and suggested that unused district campuses could alleviate space constraints.

publicComments.name: Michelle Buetler
publicComments.summary: A teacher expressed concern that school cannot function normally under all the restrictions, particularly for primary and special education students, and encouraged creative solutions such as weekly in-person check-ins and leveraging teachers who prefer to work from home.

publicComments.name: Ceri Phillips
publicComments.summary: A parent of a first grader and incoming kindergartner stressed that learning loss is a long-term concern and that 85% of parents want their children back in school, urging the district to think creatively about safe in-person options.

publicComments.name: Ali Gilmore
publicComments.summary: A parent of a student with an IEP asked for more detail about plans for special education accommodations, therapy services, and whether IEP modifications or outside service providers are being considered.

publicComments.name: Heather Morey
publicComments.summary: A PE Plus representative thanked the district for its partnership during the pandemic and expressed readiness to support the district in whatever form next year takes.

publicComments.name: Patricia Tez
publicComments.summary: A community member from North Fair Oaks asked about school lunch continuity during distance learning, how teachers would identify students suffering abuse at home, how non-English-speaking parents would be supported with technology, and what mental health support would be available for students.

publicComments.name: Victor Hernandez
publicComments.summary: The executive director of PCC School Age Childcare offered the program's support to the district for child care needs, noting they had successfully supported distance learning and were ready to expand capacity at a new campus if needed.

publicComments.name: Karina Perez
publicComments.summary: A parent from Garfield Community School advocated for Option 1 (full distance learning), arguing that Option 2 is unworkable given parent hesitancy and that Option 3 places too much burden on teachers.

publicComments.name: Daniel Hecht
publicComments.summary: A parent of an upcoming first grader at Adelante expressed serious concerns about education quality in the fall and asked how many parents have expressed interest in withdrawing from public school.

publicComments.name: Marlena Goanna
publicComments.summary: A special education instructional assistant described the physical care needs of her students, including diapering and managing runners, explained how distance learning had produced remarkable progress for her students this year, and expressed worry about regression and challenges with health protocols when students return.

publicComments.name: Carrie Schmidt
publicComments.summary: A bilingual teacher with 25 years at Hoover acknowledged the trauma of the transition to distance learning, emphasized the importance of social-emotional monitoring, expressed concern about students who rely on school as a safe space, and personally favored a hybrid model while acknowledging the virus constrains all options.

publicComments.name: Christina Lee
publicComments.summary: Began to comment on redistricting for the Selby Lane area but was redirected to agenda item 13.1.

publicComments.name: Keith
publicComments.summary: Also noted his comment was about redistricting and was redirected to agenda item 13.1.

publicComments.name: Kathryn Stewart
publicComments.summary: Asked the board to reconsider allowing out-of-district transfers into the Mandarin immersion program, especially given the shift to distance learning, and requested larger/clearer boundary maps for future presentations.

publicComments.name: Juana Martinez Gonzalez
publicComments.summary: Thanked the district for its COVID-19 support, shared her experience as a working parent navigating distance learning, expressed concern about communication barriers for non-English-speaking parents, and requested a meeting with board trustees similar to the one held with the county Office of Education.

publicComments.name: Maria Stockton
publicComments.summary: Congratulated classified employees during Classified Employees Week, thanked Alisa MacAvoy for help obtaining a city proclamation recognizing teachers and staff, and expressed appreciation to Wendy for the collaborative CSEA contract negotiations.

publicComments.name: Raquel Gonzalez
publicComments.summary: Expressed disappointment that the community's overwhelming input on the name for Adelante Selby was not accepted, and asked why the recommended name was not chosen.

Bond Program Consent Items (Action Required)

Discussion of School Attendance Boundary Change for Neighborhood Schools

publicComments.name: Christina Lee
publicComments.summary: A Kentfield Commons resident explained that her neighborhood only has access via Woodside Road, making Garfield School very difficult to reach, and urged the board to assign Kentfield Commons to the closer Henry Ford School.

publicComments.name: Keith Simmons
publicComments.summary: As a Kentfield Commons resident, echoed support for assigning the neighborhood to Henry Ford rather than Garfield, citing proximity and the ability to safely walk or bike.

publicComments.name: Kathryn Stewart
publicComments.summary: Asked for current enrollment figures for Orion/John Gill/Mandarin immersion, questioned whether the 2026 decline projections account for potential COVID baby boom, and noted that projections don't yet include anticipated birth rate changes.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
publicComments.summary: Urged the board to prioritize equity in boundary changes, cautioned against overloading lower-resourced schools like Garfield, and expressed preference for Scenario 2 which better distributes students to underutilized schools like Clifford and Roy Cloud.

publicComments.name: Lisa Gianangeli
publicComments.summary: Thanked the board for raising capacity and grandfathering questions, sought clarification on whether students in the former John Gill area assigned to Clifford could remain at Clifford through 8th grade rather than transitioning to Kennedy.

publicComments.name: Jacqueline Rodriguez
publicComments.summary: A Hoover parent and Innovate Public Schools parent leader thanked the district for its COVID support, raised concerns about ELD access for English learner students during distance learning, and requested a meeting with board trustees.

publicComments.name: Elizabeth Flegel
publicComments.summary: Asked that her small neighborhood near Sulzbach Park (adjacent to the Henry Ford boundary) be considered together with the Henry Ford area in any redistricting, and noted the large enrollment discrepancy between Adelante Selby and other K-5 schools in the presentation.

publicComments.name: Jenny Overby
publicComments.summary: Requested that Clifford's current school-of-choice enrollment be considered in projections, and asked the board to review actual classroom capacity accounting for special day classes, resource rooms, child care rooms, and technology/science rooms.

publicComments.name: Ann Heinichek
publicComments.summary: Urged the board to prioritize socioeconomic diversity in boundary decisions, calling this an opportunity to create less segregated schools, and asked for demographic and economic data to be included in future presentations.

publicComments.name: Devin Mullane
publicComments.summary: Echoed the call for diversity, raised concerns about the feasibility of the Peet's Harbor area being assigned to Clifford, and questioned why Hoover is maintained near 80% capacity while other schools run at 50%.

publicComments.name: Bill Brown
publicComments.summary: Stressed the need for a comprehensive, verified classroom-by-classroom capacity inventory before the redistricting exercise can be completed accurately, and offered to help compile such an inventory.

publicComments.name: Vanessa De la Pascua
publicComments.summary: Speaking for the Atherwood neighborhood including Kentfield Commons, explained that residents only have access via Woodside Road and urged the board to recognize that walking to Garfield requires crossing Woodside Road and El Camino, making Henry Ford a far safer and more accessible option.

publicComments.name: Thomas
publicComments.summary: A Roosevelt parent praised the school's leadership but argued that Roosevelt has been disproportionately burdened with additional students while underenrolled schools like Roy Cloud and Clifford are underutilized, and expressed frustration that wealthier neighborhoods had more influence over prior school closure decisions.

items.title: Discussion on COVID-19

publicComments.name: Pam Barish
publicComments.summary: A 29-year kindergarten teacher reminded the board to consider teacher health, family obligations, and well-being when planning school reopening, urging that what is best for teachers be weighed alongside what is best for students.

publicComments.name: Mohamed Tabrizi
publicComments.summary: A Roy Cloud parent urged the district to prioritize more effective technology platforms such as Zoom and Seesaw over Google Hangouts for distance learning, citing frustrating experiences with the mandated Google platform.

publicComments.name: Michelle Buetler
publicComments.summary: A teacher encouraged the district to inform parents not to spend money on technology devices, highlighted the value of Spanish-language social media communications, and urged mindfulness of the disparities between English- and Spanish-speaking families.

publicComments.name: Michelle Ortez
publicComments.summary: A teacher and parent expressed concern about proposed changes to social distancing ratios, stating that distance learning is the safest model and urging the board to prioritize the health of teachers and students over a return to campus.

publicComments.name: Michelle Torrido
publicComments.summary: A middle school teacher shared her positive experience mastering online platforms and achieving 90% student participation, and expressed her personal preference for continuing distance learning rather than policing social distancing rules in a classroom.

publicComments.name: Kathryn Stewart
publicComments.summary: Stated she plans to keep her child home until a vaccine is available, argued that no loss of life is acceptable, and suggested the district begin sign-ups now for families who want to remain fully remote so classroom space can be better allocated.

publicComments.name: Devin Mullane
publicComments.summary: Raised concerns about teacher workload for mirroring in-person and online lessons simultaneously, suggested alternative scheduling options like extended days or Saturday sessions, and asked whether the district has realized any cost savings from campus closures.

publicComments.name: Michelle Smith
publicComments.summary: Expressed gratitude for district efforts, raised concerns about the impact of distance learning on dual immersion programs for both Spanish-speaking and non-Spanish-speaking families who lack support at home.

publicComments.name: Maria Stockton
publicComments.summary: Asked whether instructional assistants would receive the same professional development training as teachers, raised questions about equipment access for aides, first aid and PPE training costs, temperature screening equipment, and whether the school day could be extended to compensate for lost learning time.

publicComments.name: Robin Estrada
publicComments.summary: Highlighted recurring problems with broken or locked bathrooms and non-functioning soap dispensers at Roosevelt School, urging the district to ensure functional hand-washing facilities as part of any campus reopening plan.

publicComments.name: Shawna Hackworth
publicComments.summary: Requested the district implement parental content controls or a tool like Bark for Schools on district Chromebooks, and asked for basic technology training for young students who are being asked to use Google Slides and Docs for the first time.

The meeting proceeded: Call to Order/Roll Call, Welcome/Pledge, Changes to Agenda, Approval of Agenda, then Staff Recognition (added item), then Item 6 (Action Items — bond consent), then bond action item 7.1 (not on posted agenda but taken up), then Item 8.1 (COVID-19 at-home learning discussion with public comment), then Item 10.1 (Resolution of Emergency re COVID-19 — corresponds to posted agenda Section 6 item), then reports/adjournment. publicComments.name: Pablo Lopez
publicComments.summary: Asked about the lack of formal special education and preschool distance learning plans and due dates, and raised concern about regression in students with special needs.

publicComments.name: Emily Yonts
publicComments.summary: Submitted written comment asking how after-school programs can support students virtually given that student school emails cannot accept outside addresses.

publicComments.name: Michelle Butler
publicComments.summary: Thanked district staff and leadership, acknowledged the difficulty of serving special education students in distance learning, and expressed hope that teacher dedication would be considered in compensation discussions.

publicComments.summary: Asked about getting a consolidated list of student login credentials for home use, expressed preference for Zoom over Google Meet, and asked whether out-of-district transfers for Mandarin immersion could resume if the district returns to LCFF funding.
